Historically, Swiss freeports, including the Geneva Freeport, operated with minimal government oversight, fostering concerns about illicit activities. A 1995 discovery of looted antiquities stored in the Geneva Freeport highlighted regulatory gaps, prompting scrutiny. In 2003, Swiss customs uncovered 200 stolen Egyptian artifacts, including mummies, at the Freeport, leading to the introduction of the Federal Act on the International Transfer of Cultural Property (CPTA) and Switzerland’s ratification of the 1970 UNESCO Convention. A 2005 ordinance mandated the declaration of origin, ownership, and value of imported cultural property, while a 2009 law required Freeport operators to maintain detailed inventories accessible to customs officials. These measures aimed to combat money laundering, regulate cultural property, and ensure transparency in Freeport operations, marking a significant shift in Swiss regulatory practices.

The former Grand Midland Hotel, designed by Sir George Gilbert Scott and opened in 1873, closed in 1935 and later served as British Rail offices. In 2004, Harry Handelsman's company was granted planning permission to redevelop the building, resulting in 67 apartments in the upper levels. The 245-room St Pancras Renaissance Hotel reopened in 2011 and remains under Handelsman's ownership.

The *Aslaug* is a modern Dutch-style barge that revitalized the tradition of small, versatile craft on French waterways. Ordered by private owners after years of cruising in a motor yacht, it reflects the Netherlands' historic *tjalk* and *aak* designs, which navigated Dutch canals in the 1920s–30s with 20–80 ton cargo capacities. At 15 meters, it is far smaller than the original 38-meter Freycinet barges built for French canals. Designed by Euroship Services (ESS) in the Netherlands and constructed by Ament Metaalbewerking—a Dutch metalwork firm specializing in custom vessels up to 25 meters—the *Aslaug* blends heritage with modern craftsmanship.

A church first existed in the parish before 1120. The current visible structure of the Church of St Mary the Virgin, Gamlingay, primarily dates back to the 13th century, with the square tower constructed from field stones and local ironstone containing eight bells. The South aisle and South chapel date to around 1490, while the North porch was added in the 14th century. The church underwent extensive remodeling in the 14th and 15th centuries, although the original 13th-century elements remain largely intact.

The great spotted woodpecker's call is a sharp "kik". When disturbed, this call may be repeated as a wooden, rattling "krrarraarr". The courtship call, "gwīg", is mostly given during display flights. Drumming on dead trees and branches serves to maintain contact between paired adults and to advertise territory.

According to Robin McGrath, Inuit stories suggest a history of conflict between the Inuit and the Dene, potentially stemming from trade disputes or raids for women. One of the better-known battles was recorded by British explorer Samuel Hearne, who in 1771 became the first European to explore the Coppermine River region. During this expedition, Matonabbee, leader of Hearne's Chipewyan Dene guides, and his companions massacred a Copper Inuit group at Bloody Falls, an event that deeply traumatized Hearne. This narrative, however, has been questioned in recent years, with some suggesting it unfairly characterizes Inuit-Dene relations. Further exploration of the Coppermine River region did not take place until the period of 1820-1853, which included the Sir John Franklin expeditions of 1821 and 1825. In 1847, John Rae, a Hudson's Bay Company surgeon and surveyor, encountered Copper Inuit at Rae River, and later at Cape Flinders and Stromness Bay in 1851.

Equinox Minerals was formed in January 2004 through a merger between Equinox and Equinox Resources, and was incorporated as a Canadian company the same month. The company began operations in Zambia in 1996. Prior to Equinox's acquisition of the Lumwana project in 1999, at least ten different companies had failed to develop it. The Lumwana project, located west of the Copperbelt, 65km from the city of Solwezi, covers two major copper mines and 1,355 square kilometers. In January 2010, Equinox acquired the Jabal Sayid project in Saudi Arabia, with an annual ore production capacity of 60,000 tonnes of copper expected to commence in 2012. In February 2011, Equinox launched a $4.8 billion hostile takeover bid for Lundin Mining, who at the same time was attempting a $9 billion merger with Inmet Mining. In April 2011, Equinox rejected a takeover offer from Minmetals Resources (MMR), but later that month accepted a bid from Barrick Gold Corporation, who announced the acquisition of Equinox for C$7.2 billion ($7.4 billion) on April 11, 2011.

The Pakistan Muslim League (N) (PML-N) advocates for conservative liberalism and economic liberalism, emphasizing free markets and individual achievement as key drivers of economic prosperity. The party supports deregulation across the economic order and has a strong base in capitalism. In 1991, the PML-N government established the National Highway Authority and later inaugurated the M2 Motorway in 1997, highlighting its commitment to infrastructure development as part of its economic policies.

During World War II, the United States engaged in both conventional and unconventional warfare. Unconventional warfare was critical in the China-Burma-India (CBI) theater, North Africa, and Europe, supplementing conventional forces. In the CBI theater, Air Commandos pioneered air mobility operations behind enemy lines. The Office of Strategic Services (OSS) conducted clandestine operations, including psychological warfare and parachuting agents into enemy territory. The US fought against Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan. After the war, the Air Force focused on acquiring German technology.

The Long-billed Curlew, North America's largest shorebird, experienced significant population declines due to agricultural intensification over the past 150 years. New research confirmed the species' vulnerability, leading to its downlisting from "Near Threatened" to "Least Concern" by the IUCN in 2008. However, declines appear to be ongoing in some areas.

Edmund Stonelake was elected unopposed to the Aberdare Urban District Council in 1904, retaining his seat until 1922. He became chairman in 1909–10 and was a prominent advocate for improved housing before World War I. Despite his innocence, he resigned in 1922 after a corruption scandal involving council officials (1919–22). His public service continued with his appointment as a J.P. in 1928. (62 words)

In the German Empire, the Schwetz district formed the **Marienwerder 5** Reichstag constituency, contested between **German and Polish** candidates, with winners prevailing by **narrow majorities** in closely fought elections.

Mick Connaboy was granted time by the SFA at the end of the season to consider his next move, and just 12 days later, he signed for Cowdenbeath. During the early months of the season, Connaboy showcased his versatility by occupying both midfield and defensive positions. In October, he scored his first goal of the season in a match against Falkirk. The game was notable for an unusual incident involving Cowdenbeath’s goalkeeper, who was forced to play outfield after an injury crisis left the team short of substitutes. Despite Connaboy’s contributions, Cowdenbeath took disciplinary action against him in March, citing a breach of club rules, which led to a temporary suspension.

"The Mark of the Angels – Miserere" was widely released in France on June 26, 2013, initially showing in 288 theaters. By its second week, the number dropped to 156 theaters. The film is expected to premiere in Belgium later in 2013, with sales handled by Pathé International.

Barbara Jatta began her Vatican career in 1996 as the director of the Cabinet of Prints at the Vatican Library, a position she held for two decades. In June 2016, she was appointed deputy director of the Vatican Museums under Antonio Paolucci, who had served as director since 2007 and was known for his extensive renovations of the museums. On December 20, 2016, Pope Francis announced Jatta’s appointment as the new director of the Vatican Museums, a role she officially assumed on January 1, 2017, becoming the first woman to lead the institution in its history. As director, Jatta oversees the vast papal art collections, including Michelangelo’s iconic Sistine Chapel ceiling, and manages the museums, which generate millions of euros annually from tourism. Recognizing the importance of balancing tradition and innovation, Jatta has worked to enhance the visitor experience by addressing overcrowding, introducing new entry systems, and encouraging visitors to explore lesser-known artworks beyond the Sistine Chapel. She has also emphasized the use of digital tools to promote and educate about the museums, building on her earlier work in modernizing the Vatican Library’s online presence. In March 2017, Jatta presided over her first exhibition as director, titled *Dilectissimo fratri Caesario Symmachus*, further cementing her leadership in this historic role.

Terry Pratchett cultivated a large and dedicated fanbase, primarily through his *Discworld* series, which led to dedicated conventions. The first Discworld Convention was held in 1996. Pratchett himself often served as the guest of honour at these events. The publication of a new book was often accompanied by signing tours, with queues known to span multiple floors of venues due to Pratchett's dedication to each fan. His readership was diverse, encompassing all ages and genders. Pratchett received a large amount of mail from his fans and acknowledged their importance to him, stating, "My fans are everything to me."

According to the 2010 census, the population of Talent, Oregon, was 4,538, residing in 2,001 households and 1,178 families. The population density was 3,151.4 inhabitants per square mile. There were 2,149 housing units, with an average density of 1,492.4 per square mile. The racial makeup of the city was 84.0% White. While the census data doesn't explicitly state the percentage of the population that was Hispanic or Latino, context 4 indicates that communities in the Rogue Valley, such as Phoenix, saw significant increases in their Latino populations.

The **Goliath stick insect** (*Eurycnema goliath*) employs **passive camouflage** and **plant mimicry**, resembling leaves or branches through its bright green coloration and elongated shape. It enhances this disguise with a **swaying body movement**, mimicking the motion of wind-blown foliage. This adaptation helps the insect **avoid detection by predators**, blending seamlessly into its arboreal environment.  

(59 words)

Bemis, Tennessee, was founded in 1900 by Judson Moss Bemis, president of the Bemis Brothers Bag Company. Bemis sought to build a cotton mill in the South, near cotton fields, a reliable labor source, and accessible rail lines. Madison County was selected, and its legislative body agreed to purchase 300 acres for $6,000 to turn over to the Bemis Bag Company. Construction began in 1900 on a site located on the Illinois Central Railroad line. The first mill, with a 21,000-spindle capacity, was completed and operational by June 1, 1901.

The Aloyseum, established in 1913, began with a donation by Italian Jesuit Fr. Chiapi, who contributed around 2,000 minerals, herbarium, and Roman coins. Among its notable exhibits is the first car in Mangalore, a French-made De Dion, imported in 1906 by P. F. X. Saldanha of Highland Coffee Works. The museum also houses a vast collection of domestic and agricultural utensils from bygone eras, alongside unique artifacts like the first generator installed in the college.

In 1998, following the overturning of James Lambert's conviction by federal Judge Stewart Dalzell, the federal court system debated whether Lambert's claims of prosecutorial misconduct and withheld evidence warranted a new trial. After the initial ruling was overturned, Lambert appealed for a hearing regarding the second overturning of the verdict, but the Pennsylvania Supreme Court ultimately rejected his claims, stating that the disputed evidence was not material and would not have changed the outcome of his trial. The case was returned to the Lancaster County Court system in 1997, and a third trial was scheduled for May 1998. During this trial, overseen by Judge Stewart Stengel, the court ruled that evidence from the 1997 hearing would not be admissible. Witnesses, including key figures from the original case, were called to testify against Lambert, but a detective's testimony denied any wrongdoing in the handling of evidence. Meanwhile, a federal appeals court intervened, ordering that Lambert be retried or released within four months, further complicating the proceedings. The third trial marked a critical juncture in the long-running legal battle over Lambert's conviction.

Broadhall Way is the stadium where Stevenage Football Club plays its home matches. Previously, it was home to Stevenage Town and Stevenage Athletic. Following the bankruptcy of the town's former club, the stadium was not used for three years. The newly formed Stevenage Borough had planned to play Hitchin Town Youth at Broadhall Way in November 1976. However, plans were disrupted when the ex-chairman of Stevenage Athletic, who was also the stadium lease-holder, desecrated the playing surface by digging a trench across the full length of the pitch. The club eventually moved into Broadhall Way in 1980 after the council re-purchased the stadium, allowing the football club to become its tenant.

Sharon Jarvis's equestrian journey began at the age of five when her parents, yielding to her persistent requests, bought her a pony. She started competing in para-equestrian events in 2006, classified as a Grade III rider due to loss of movement and strength in her lower left side. Her Australian debut was in 2007 at the FEI World Para-Equestrian Dressage Championships in Gloucestershire, England. She was then selected for the 2008 Paralympic Games in Beijing. In 2010, at the World Equestrian Games in Lexington, Kentucky, Jarvis won two bronze medals in the Individual Grade 3 Championship Test and the Grade 3 Freestyle Test.

**Saint Vincent Chapel, Taiwan** was built in Father Vincent R. Capodanno's Taiwanese mission territory as his family's first-choice memorial. Within four months of his 1967 death, nearly $4,000 (equivalent to $40,000 in 2024) was raised by organizations like The American Legion, Veterans of Foreign Wars, Knights of Columbus, and the Marine Corps League. Donald R. Marshall chaired the fundraising committee, with retired Navy Chaplain Eli Takesian as honorary chairman. After delays, the chapel was completed between 1993 and 1995 in Taiwan, where Capodanno had served the Hakka people as a Maryknoll missionary.

After World War II, the U.S. Office of Strategic Services' **Art Looting Investigation Unit (ALIU)** investigated Nazi art plundering, including the **Führermuseum** planned for Linz. The ALIU produced detailed reports, later consolidated into four, with the fourth—covering Hitler's Linz museum—written by **S. Lane Faison**. The reports exposed Nazi looting networks. Under the **1943 London Declaration**, which invalidated German art acquisitions in occupied territories, the **Allied forces** restituted looted art, aided by the **Roberts Commission** and **MFAA**.

**Mirror particles** were first considered by Matej Pavšič in 1974, proposing that nature could be exactly symmetric under space inversion if internal and external transformations were combined. The idea originated with Lee and Yang’s 1956 paper on parity violation, later expanded by Kobzarev, Okun, and Pomeranchuk in 1966, linking mirror matter to neutral kaon decays. Mirror matter models, including exact parity models (SM-SM'), explain dark matter and unify particle symmetries. These models remain significant in contemporary physics for addressing mass hierarchies, neutrinos, and quantum gravity.

The NIT2 gene in humans, which is identical to omega-amidase, is predominantly expressed in the cytosol of various tissues. Overexpression of NIT2 has been shown to inhibit cell proliferation and growth in colon cancer cells, suggesting its potential role as a tumor suppressor. Studies indicate that downregulation of NIT2 in colon cancer cells leads to decreased proliferation and induces cell cycle arrest. Additionally, NIT2/omega-amidase plays a crucial role in controlling the concentration of potentially toxic substrates, such as alpha-ketoglutaramate and alpha-ketosuccinamate, by converting them into biologically useful metabolites, thereby mitigating their toxicity.

In addition to her WNBA career, Brittney Sykes is a member of the Israeli team Hapoel Petah Tikva, where she plays during the offseason. The team competes in the Israeli league. Sykes is joined by her former Syracuse University teammate, Alexis Peterson, who played for the Seattle Storm during her 2017 rookie season and is currently with the Las Vegas Aces.

**International perspective**  
Halloween and Halloween-like traditions, such as Austria's *Kurbisfest im Retzer Land* (Pumpkin Festival), are celebrated worldwide, though the modern commercialized haunted attraction is a uniquely American invention. The haunted house industry, which began gaining traction in the U.S. in the late 20th century, started exporting globally in the past few decades, particularly to Europe and Asia. Theme parks like Universal Studios and Disney have played a major role in globalizing Halloween, with Universal hosting *Halloween Horror Nights* in Florida, California, Japan, and Singapore, while Disney mounts *Mickey's Not-So-Scary Halloween* events in Paris, Hong Kong, and Tokyo. Six Flags locations also celebrate *Fright Fest*, expanding the trend. Overseas operators, such as Universal Studios Singapore (launching its first *Horror Nights* in recent years) and Hollywood-inspired haunted houses supplying films like *ExMortis*, have adopted American-style attractions, reflecting the holiday's growing worldwide appeal.

The Rachel Carson Center (RCC) hosts seminars, workshops, and public events to share research on environment-society interactions. Its outreach includes the **Environment & Society Portal**, an open-access gateway for global audiences, developed with the **Deutsches Museum** for exhibitions. The RCC offers the **Doctoral Program Environment and Society** through **LMU Munich**, focusing on interdisciplinary environmental research. Activities also involve collaborations with institutions like the **Kate Hamburger Kolleg**, fostering academic and public engagement.

Nutrition plays a crucial role in nail health, with foods rich in protein, magnesium, and biotin potentially strengthening brittle fingernails. Small studies involving 45 participants showed that 2.5 mg of biotin daily improved symptoms in 91% after six weeks to seven months.

Smith College has 285 professors across 41 academic departments and programs, with a student-faculty ratio of 9:1. Notably, it is the first women's college in the U.S. to graduate an all-women class of engineers, offering a Bachelor of Science (S.B.) degree through its Picker Engineering Program.

Olavo de Carvalho (1947–2022), a self-proclaimed philosopher and far-right conspiracy theorist, became one of the most influential figures in Jair Bolsonaro's administration. Bolsonaro engaged with Carvalho's ideas as early as 2014, and their live YouTube chats began between 2014 and 2016. By 2017, Carvalho was widely depicted as Bolsonaro's "ideologue," a title he refuted that same year.

Archaeologists believe the area now known as Sulphur Dell was once a Native American settlement dating back to at least the Pleistocene epoch, over twelve thousand years ago. The site likely held ritual significance, with ceremonies involving the mineral-rich springs, which early settlers referred to as "French Lick." These settlers, arriving in the early 1800s, used the land for farming and commercialized the springs for their perceived medicinal properties. Known also as French Lick Springs, the area became a popular retreat, but by the mid-19th century, entrepreneurs had restricted access to the springs. By 1866, Sulphur Dell had gained prominence as a baseball field, where amateur teams played, marking the beginning of its association with the sport.

Methedrone, a synthetic cathinone, is reported to produce powerful psychostimulant and entactogenic effects, with anecdotal and case reports likening its psychological impact to that of banned substances such as MDMA, cocaine, and amphetamines. Psychological effects associated with methedrone use include agitation, anxiety, paranoia, and, in severe cases, psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions. Physically, methedrone can cause increased heart rate, elevated blood pressure, sweating, jaw clenching, and dehydration, with excessive use potentially leading to severe complications like hyperthermia, seizures, and cardiovascular issues. Preclinical studies have primarily focused on its effects in rodents, particularly mice and rats. In mice, methedrone has been shown to enhance nicotine-induced behavioral sensitization, increase anxiety-like behaviors, and exhibit pro-cognitive effects in specific paradigms. Additionally, it influences oxidative stress in the brain and alters serotonin and dopamine transporter functions. Legally, methedrone is classified as a controlled substance in many jurisdictions under international drug control conventions, reflecting its similarity in pharmacological properties to other banned psychostimulants.

The Florida Gators football team was led by Jim McElwain until October 29, 2017, when Randy Shannon was named interim head coach. The Gators finished the 2017 season with a 4–7 overall record and a 3–5 mark in SEC play.

"Strange Magic" holds an approval rating of 19% on Rotten Tomatoes, based on 62 critic reviews, with an average rating of 3.8/10 as of October 2021. The critical consensus indicates that the film, while visually impressive and featuring lively renditions of pop songs, lacks the emotional depth and ambition of other animated features. On Metacritic, it has a score of 25, based on 20 critic reviews, suggesting generally unfavorable reviews. Audiences polled by CinemaScore gave the film a grade of "B."

The name "Multnomah" originates from the Multnomah Indians, part of the Chinookan tribe residing near Sauvie Island. The existence of a historical chief named Multnomah is debated, with some oral histories from local tribes referencing him. However, many Oregon historians, particularly in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, regarded Chief Multnomah as a fictional character, a view supported by the Oregon Historical Society's presidents during that time. Despite this, writings like Frederick Balch's "The Bridge of the Gods" suggest a historical basis for the figure, potentially inspired by the chief Kiesno.

In 2009, Flawless auditioned for *Britain’s Got Talent* (Series 3) in London, dazzling judges with their high-energy street dance routine. Amanda Holden called them "jaw-droppingly outstanding," while Piers Morgan deemed them "electrifying." They received four yeses, advancing shortly before ventriloquist Gareth Oliver.

In Bogotá, the average weekday public transit commute takes 97 minutes, with 32% of riders spending over two hours daily in transit. Commuters typically wait an average of 20 minutes at stops or stations, though 40% experience waits exceeding this time. The average distance traveled in a single trip is 8 km, while 12% of riders journey more than 12 km in one direction. These statistics highlight the challenges faced by Bogotá’s public transit users, reflecting the city’s extensive traffic congestion and the reliance on systems like TransMilenio and SITP for daily commutes.

The village of Barak, Kyrgyzstan's only exclave in the Fergana Valley, has a population of approximately 627. Surrounded by Uzbek territory, it lies on the road from Osh to Khodjaabad, about 4 kilometers northwest of the Kyrgyz–Uzbek border. Administratively, Barak is part of the Kara-Suu District in Kyrgyzstan's Osh Region.

Salesian College and the John Paul II School merged in August 2011 to create St John Bosco College, located in the Wandsworth Borough. St John Bosco College officially opened on 1 September 2011 at the former John Paul II School site. The college later moved to the old Surrey Lane site in Battersea in 2015. The site has also been used as a filming location for the television show "The Inbetweeners" and served as art studios for the organization "The Art Academy."

Luca Pacioli, sometimes called Lucas di Burgo, was born in Borgo San Sepolcro (now Borgo Sansepolcro) in Tuscany, likely around 1445, though some sources suggest a later date as late as 1450. He was the son of Bartolomeo Pacioli and is said to have lived with the Befolci family as a child. In Sansepolcro, Pacioli received an *abbaco* education, which focused on practical knowledge for merchants and was taught in the local language rather than Latin. Around 1464, he moved to Venice, where he tutored the sons of a merchant while continuing his education and developing significant knowledge in commercial accounting. It was during this period that he wrote his first arithmetic treatise. Pacioli was a Franciscan friar between 1472 and 1475.

E.M.D.'s cover of "Save Tonight," originally by Eagle-Eye Cherry, debuted at number 5 on the Swedish singles chart. The song marked their third top 5 single. The week after its debut, it fell to number 46. In subsequent weeks, it climbed back up the chart, eventually peaking at number 3.

Ludvík Podéšť studied music composition at the Brno Conservatory, where he graduated in 1945 under the guidance of his teacher, Václav Kaprál. He later pursued musicology at Masaryk University, studying under professors Janáček and Šebánek. Podéšť held the position of music director at the Czech Radio studio in Brno and served as the conductor of the Radost Youth Choir. From 1950 to 1953, he was the director of the Vít Nejedlý Army Artistic Ensemble in Prague, succeeding conductor Václav Štěpán. Between 1958 and 1961, he worked at Czechoslovak Television, focusing on television music production after 1961. He lived in Prague from 1966 until his death, and his wife was a music teacher.

The initial investigation involving Hussain took place in New York, where he was linked to a Medicaid fraud scheme. Osman, another figure in the 21 July 2005 London bombings, stated he opposed war, participated in peace rallies, and claimed the bombs were intended to highlight anger over the Iraq War rather than to kill. He denied any connection to al-Qaeda.

Valentina Cortese was born on January 1, 1923, in Milan, Italy, to Olga Cortese and Napoleone Rossi di Coenzo, a nobleman who abandoned her mother before her birth. In the late 1940s, she began a relationship with Victor de Sabata, a married conductor 30 years her senior, but moved to Hollywood to end the affair. In 1951, she married actor Richard Basehart, with whom she had a son, Jackie Basehart. The couple divorced in 1960, and Cortese never remarried. Jackie died in Milan in 2015, four years before Cortese’s own death in 2019.

The emblem of the Municipality of Freienfeld features an argent equilateral triangle with inward-curved sides inscribed in a vert shield. The three vert sectors represent the former municipalities of Trens, Stilfes, and Mauls, while the white part symbolizes Freienfeld. The emblem was granted in 1968.

Roger Love began studying vocal technique as a teenager under famed Hollywood coach Seth Riggs. At 17, while Riggs taught opera in Canada, Love temporarily took over his studio, coaching stars like Brian Wilson, Earth, Wind & Fire, and the Jacksons. He later became Riggs' junior partner before founding his own studio, attracting top clients. Initially interested in a singing career, Love shifted to coaching, influenced by these early pop clients. He now owns Roger Love Voice Studios.

Heaven Knows What received generally positive reviews from critics, holding a rating of 92% on Rotten Tomatoes based on 48 reviews, with an average rating of 7.8/10. The critical consensus highlights the film as a "grueling and rewarding" portrayal of addiction, showcasing the captivating performance of star Arielle Holmes. On Metacritic, the film has a score of 76 out of 100, based on 15 critic reviews, indicating generally favorable reviews.

As of the 2000 census, Waupaca, Wisconsin had a population of 20,740, with 8,452 households and 5,814 families residing in the county. The population density was 24 people per square mile (9.3 people/km²). There were 11,187 housing units at an average density of 13 units per square mile (5.0 units/km²). The racial makeup of the county was 97.93% White, and 0.82% of the population were Hispanic or Latino of any race.

According to the 2020 U.S. Census, Ann Arbor, Michigan, had a population of 123,851, with a population density of 4,508 inhabitants per square mile—higher than outer-ring suburbs like Livonia and Troy but lower than Detroit proper and inner-ring suburbs like Oak Park and Ferndale. The racial makeup was 65.9% White, 6.6% Black, 15.6% Asian, and 5.5% Hispanic or Latino of any race. Native American, Alaska Native, Native Hawaiian, Pacific Islander, and Other races collectively made up 0.8%, while 5.6% identified as Multiracial. Arab Americans, though a small community, include Lebanese Muslims and other groups, reflecting Metro Detroit’s broader Arab-American population. Household data from 2010 (the latest available) recorded 47,060 households.

The **Beryl oil field** is a producing conventional oil field located in shallow water in the UK North Sea, within blocks 9/13a-d. Discovered in 1972, it was named after Beryl Solomon, wife of Mobil Europe president Charles Solomon. The field's geology comprises Middle Jurassic deltaic sediments, Upper Triassic to Lower Jurassic marine sediments, and Upper Jurassic turbidites, with Beryl Sandstone as the primary reservoir.

Before establishing his kingdom, Ala-ud-Din Bahman Shah, originally known as Zafar Khan or Hasan Gangu, served as the Governor of the Deccan and a commander under the Delhi Sultanate of Muhammad bin Tughlaq. On August 3, 1347, Nazir Uddin Ismail Shah, an elderly noble who had revolted against the Delhi Sultanate, voluntarily stepped down in favor of Bahman Shah. This marked the establishment of the Bahmani Sultanate, the first independent Islamic kingdom in South India. Bahman Shah, of Turkic or possibly Afghan origin, declared his independence and set up his headquarters in Ahsanabad (modern-day Gulbarga), which served as the capital of the new state. The majority of the Bahmanid army was composed of Turkic soldiers, reflecting the founder's heritage. Bahman Shah’s coronation as Alauddin Bahman Shah Sultan was supported by the influential Chishti Sufi Shaikhs, who legitimized his rule by bestowing upon him a robe said to have been worn by the Prophet Muhammad. This symbolic gesture extended the Sufi notion of spiritual sovereignty to his political authority, solidifying his position as the ruler of the Deccan.

Keith Lambert began his professional cycling career in 1972, signing his first contract with the Falcon cycling team under the management of Ernie Clements. His first professional victory came in the Grand Prix of Essex. On 6 August 1972, Lambert competed in the Milk Race, finishing as the top British rider. In 1974, he joined the Holdsworth team, where he raced alongside notable teammates such as Les West and Colin Lewis. That same year, Lambert claimed his first British National Road Race Championship title. He also competed in the 1976 Paris-Roubaix, finishing the grueling event. In 1978, Lambert signed with the TI-Raleigh team and placed 12th at the 1979 World Championship in Valkenburg aan de Geul. He joined Weinmann in 1980, finishing second in the Druivenkoers Overijse, and later that year, he narrowly defeated Bill Nickson to win his second British National Road Race Championship in Redditch. Lambert rejoined Falcon in 1983, where he won the British Professional Circuit Race Championship, and competed in the 1982 World Championship in Goodwood. Over his 15-year professional career, spanning from 1972 to 1987, Lambert achieved 61 professional victories, cementing his legacy as one of Britain’s most accomplished cyclists.

Werner & Mertz introduced the first phosphate-free household cleaning agent under the brand name Frosch in April 1986. The company has adopted a market position focused on environmental responsibility through its brands, including Frosch, Erdal, tana PROFESSIONAL, green care PROFESSIONAL, Rorax, Emsal, and Bionicdry. Since 2009, Emsal products have contained surfactants from European cultivation, while the Erdal shoe care range has been solvent-free. This commitment to sustainability reflects Werner & Mertz's dedication to corporate social responsibility in the hygiene and cleaning product market.

Constantijn Huygens faced significant personal grief in 1638 with the death of his wife. Earlier, in 1630, he was appointed to the Council and Exchequer, where he managed the estate of the Orange family, earning an annual income of about 1000 florins. That same year, he purchased the heerlijkheid Zuilichem, acquiring the title of Heer van Zuilichem. In 1632, he was appointed as Knight of the Order of Saint-Michel by Louis XIII of France. Additionally, in 1643, Huygens was granted the honor of displaying a golden lily on a blue field in his coat of arms.

*Alfred Hitchcock Presents* is well known for its iconic title sequence, where the camera fades in on a simple line-drawing caricature of Hitchcock's rotund profile—drawn by Hitchcock himself—accompanied by Charles Gounod's *"Funeral March of a Marionette"* (suggested by composer Bernard Herrmann). Hitchcock then appears in silhouette, walking to center screen to eclipse the caricature, before greeting the audience with his signature *"Good evening."* The caricature and Gounod's theme have become indelibly associated with Hitchcock in popular culture. The anthology series, featuring mysteries and thrillers, premiered on October 2, 1955, and ran until 1965.

The Camden and Atlantic Railroad built a rail line between Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, and Atlantic City, New Jersey, in 1854. The Long-a-Coming Depot was constructed along this line and was renamed in 1867. The station closed in the 1980s due to declining passenger numbers, and in 1968, the PRSL attempted to demolish the station as part of its efforts to reduce operational costs.

Theory of Mind (ToM) is crucial for social interaction, and deficits in ToM decoding, the ability to understand others' beliefs, desires, and intentions, are seen in disorders like autism spectrum disorder (Context 3, 12). An opposite pattern of enhanced ToM is observed in some individuals vulnerable to depression, including those with a maternal history of mind-mindedness (Context 10). Past major depressive disorder may be associated with this enhanced ToM.

The fluorenone core, particularly when part of a benzofuran ring system, is a basic structural unit in many biologically active natural medicines, attracting significant research attention. Azafluorenones are representative members of this class. Onychine, polyfothine, and isoursuline exhibit antimalarial effects. Cyathocaline intercalates into DNA. Azafluorenone derivatives possess reported biological activities and are used in fluorescence applications, showing topoisomerase I inhibition.

Interstate 229 (I-229) begins at a system interchange with I-29 and US 71 in Buchanan County, Missouri. From there, I-229 heads east, meeting Route A at a half-diamond interchange that only allows access to Route A from southbound I-229. North of I-229 at this point lies the St. Joseph State Hospital, while south of I-229 is the Missouri River. The next half-diamond interchange provides access to Route 752 from southbound I-229 only. Full access to Route 752 is provided by Route 759. Note that Route 371 is not in Missouri, but in Crow Wing County, Minnesota.

Generalbezirk Estland was the last of the four districts to be formally established on December 5, 1941, on the territory of Estonia. Before its establishment, the area was under military administration by the German forces, with Reval (Tallinn) serving as its capital.

Lake Sacajawea was constructed in 1924 by dredging Fowler's Slough, a former channel of the Cowlitz River. This transformation was part of a larger city planning project led by the Long-Bell Lumber Company during Longview's founding.

Since the early 1970s, tree stumps and logs with attached roots and bark have been appearing under the retreating Mendenhall Glacier in Alaska. In the years following 1937, Hitler aimed to unify all Protestant churches in Germany under a single, Nazified structure, reflecting his earlier policy of creating a cohesive religious front that aligned with Nazi ideology. However, by 1938, he had largely abandoned the syncretist idea of Positive Christianity, which sought to blend Christian beliefs with Nazi principles. Historian Richard Weikart noted that Hitler publicly supported a Nazified version of science after this time, while Thomas Childers observed that Hitler began to embrace a more overtly pagan ideology, replacing traditional religious beliefs with a core of Nazi ideology centered on racial superiority and the survival of the fittest. Weikart characterized Hitler's belief in evolutionary ethics as simplistic, failing to account for the complexities of altruism and cooperation in human societies.

*The Marriage* is an experimental art game by Rod Humble, known for his work on *EverQuest*, *The Sims*, and *Second Life*. Elective epidural steroid injections should be performed with imaging guidance, unless contraindicated, to improve accuracy and safety. Fluoroscopy, computed tomography (CT), and ultrasound are examples of imaging modalities used. Image guidance allows for precise needle placement, identification of inadvertent punctures, and correction of needle position, improving therapy efficacy. Without imaging, incorrect needle placement can be significant, reaching up to 50% in caudal approaches. Traditionally, some physicians perform "blind" injections, relying on palpation of anatomical landmarks like the spinous process and the loss-of-resistance technique. However, studies have shown that image-guided injections are superior in reaching the correct anatomical target compared to landmark-based approaches.

**Seismic-induced ore deposits** may form in regions where fault zones facilitate hydrothermal fluid circulation, particularly in fractured or crushed rock areas, as seen in the Hizejan-Sharafabad fault zone (Context 6). A 2015 case study by Christine Howald in Nevada (Context 9) examined mineralization linked to seismic activity. The study focused on iron and calcium deposits (Context 3), identifying two separate seismic events through seismogram analysis (Context 4) and dating them using historical earthquake records (Context 2). Following this, hydrothermal fluids infiltrated the fault zone, initiating mineralization. The study concluded that mineralization began due to seismic-induced fracturing, with deposition requiring both fluid migration and chemical saturation (Context 6, 3).

The first step in preparing a hot air balloon for flight after unpacking it from its carrying bag is laying it out on the ground and connecting it to the basket and burner. A fan, often gasoline-powered, is then used to blow cold (outside) air into the envelope. This cold air partially inflates the balloon to establish its basic shape before the burner flame is aimed into the mouth, heating the air inside. A crew member stationed opposite the mouth holds a rope (crown line) tied to the apex (crown) of the envelope. The crown line's purpose is twofold: to prevent the envelope from excessive sway and to prevent it from rising before it is sufficiently buoyant. Once the balloon is upright, the pilot and passengers climb into the basket. When the pilot is ready for launch, more heat is directed into the envelope, and the balloon lifts off.

After the 2014 midterm elections, Republicans gained control of the Senate (Context 3, 12, 14), giving them the power to further influence judicial confirmations (Context 12). *The Ladies of the House* garnered positive attention from horror review websites, including Bloody Disgusting and Twitch Film. Bloody Disgusting noted that director John Wildman and co-writer Justina Walford "have their heads and hearts in the right place and can only get better from here," ultimately feeling they liked the film more than initially thought. Twitch Film praised the film as "a confetti explosion of candy apple color, spinning vinyl records, rockabilly busty babes, blood spatter, and a whole lotta retro love."

On December 2, 2015, the Oakland Athletics traded Drew Pomeranz, along with minor league pitcher Jose Torres and a player to be named later, to the San Diego Padres in exchange for Yonder Alonso and Marc Rzepczynski. Pomeranz, who had approached the Padres about becoming a starter, secured a spot in their rotation. In 2016, he was named to the National League All-Star team, posting a 2.47 ERA and leading the NL with a .184 batting average against in the first half.

**Ancient forest uncovered**  
As the Mendenhall Glacier near Juneau, Alaska, retreats, ancient tree stumps and logs—some still rooted and bearing bark—have been exposed since at least 2011. Preserved by glacial ice for over two millennia, these trees, now lying in a gravelly mix of churned-up stone, provide insights into past climates, including precipitation and atmospheric conditions. Scientists determine their ages by analyzing preserved remains, with Cathy Conner reporting the most recent stumps to be 1,200–1,400 years old and the oldest around 2,350 years old. The findings help reconstruct historical climate patterns as the glacier continues to shrink.

Analysis of the GW170104 signal indicated it resulted from the coalescence of a pair of stellar-mass black holes. The black holes had inferred component masses of approximately 31.2 and 19.4 solar masses. The merger created a black hole of about 142 solar masses and released energy equivalent to around 8 solar masses in the form of gravitational waves. The event occurred at a distance of 490 megaparsecs (1.6 billion light years) from Earth. The peak luminosity of GW170104 was 3.0+0.7−1.6×10^49 watts.

As of the 2000 census, Middleport, Pennsylvania, had a population of 458, with 197 households and 126 families. The population density was 849 people per square mile, and there were 215 housing units, resulting in an average housing density of 500.0 per square mile. The racial composition was 98.3% White, 0.2% Native American, 0.2% from other races, and 1.3% Hispanic or Latino of any race.

The great spotted woodpecker is hunted by woodland birds of prey such as the Eurasian sparrowhawk and the northern goshawk. It serves as a host to the blood-feeding fly Carnus hemapterus and may harbor the internal parasite Prosthorhynchus transversus. Additionally, it can be affected by the protozoan Toxoplasma gondii, which causes toxoplasmosis, and is the favored host of the tapeworm Anomotaenia brevis.

Fitchville Township is governed by a three-member board of trustees, with members elected for a four-year term. Elections occur during general elections, with two trustees elected in presidential election years and one trustee elected in gubernatorial election years. The terms for the board of trustees begin on January 1 following the election. Additionally, a fiscal officer, responsible for financial matters, is elected for a four-year term, with their term also commencing on January 1 after the election.

Donald Trump opposed international climate cooperation, pledging in May 2016 to "cancel" the Paris Agreement, adopted by nearly 200 countries in December 2015 to cut carbon emissions. Within his first 100 days in office, he vowed to withdraw the U.S. from the accord, arguing it unfairly burdened American taxpayers while favoring countries like China and India. Trump claimed he would, at minimum, seek to renegotiate the deal, calling it economically harmful to the U.S. He falsely asserted that the agreement allowed "foreign bureaucrats" to control U.S. energy use, despite its reliance on voluntary national pledges. Trump framed the accord as detrimental to American energy production, particularly coal, and criticized its emissions targets as disadvantaging the U.S.

In late 2004, InterPride decided to hold the second World Pride parade in Jerusalem for 2006, despite significant opposition. The Israeli group chosen to host this event was the Jerusalem Open House. In response, QUIT! launched a campaign urging a boycott of World Pride, citing the inappropriateness of holding such an event in a city with a 30-foot concrete wall. The World Pride event was ultimately postponed to August 2006 due to concerns over the political climate and local opposition. In March 2006, the Palestinian gay women's group ASWAT decided not to participate, highlighting the ongoing suffering of Palestinians under occupation. The International Gay and Lesbian Human Rights Commission (IGLHRC) also expressed concerns, noting that many LGBTI individuals faced travel restrictions and conditions that limited their ability to attend World Pride 2006.

Youssef Shaaban was married four times. His first marriage was to actress Laila Taher, lasting four years. His second wife was Nadia Chirine, the daughter of Fawzia Fuad of Egypt; they had a daughter named Sinai. His third wife was actress Seham Fathi. In 1989, Shaaban married Kuwaiti national Iman Shreaan, with whom he had two children: a daughter, Zainab, and a son, Murad.

In **December 2020**, the *Washington Examiner* reported that **Paul Teller**, previously **Special Assistant to the President for Legislative Affairs**, was appointed by **Vice President Mike Pence** to serve as **Executive Director** of **Advancing American Freedom**, a policy advocacy group. In this role, Teller led strategic initiatives and maintained relationships with **Congressional conservatives and outside groups**. His official White House title remained **Special Assistant to the President**, and his work was associated with the **Trump administration**.  

*(Word count: 80)*

The 2015 Tour of Chongming Island World Cup race started in Shanghai and primarily took place on flat, wide roads. The course featured gentle corners, allowing for high-speed racing. Initially, the route followed the roads leading to a 10-kilometer underwater tunnel under the Yangtze River, which the peloton used to reach Changxing Island. After exiting the tunnel, riders crossed the 8-kilometer Shanghai Yangtze River Bridge to access Chongming Island, where the race continued on familiar terrain conducive to sprint finishes.

Much of the Oregon and California (O&C) Railroad land was rugged and unsuitable for farming, but its rich timber resources significantly increased its value, with timber companies willing to pay far more than the $2.50 per acre limit set by the land grant terms. Edward Harriman, president of the Southern Pacific Railroad, which controlled the O&C lands, hired Stephen A. Douglas Puter to circumvent these requirements. Puter recruited individuals, often from urban areas, to fraudulently register as "settlers" for 160-acre parcels. Once registered, the parcels were transferred to timber companies or speculators in violation of the grant's conditions, allowing large-scale accumulation of valuable timberland and bypassing the intended small-scale settlement.

Bride kidnapping in Kyrgyzstan, though illegal since 1994, persists and is debated as a tradition. Experts argue arranged marriage, not abduction, is the historical norm. Historically, voluntary elopements, often mistaken for kidnappings, allowed couples to escape arranged marriages, contrasting with today's non-consensual abductions.

Mary Elizabeth Wood was born on August 22, 1861, in the township of Elba, near Batavia, New York, to Edward Farmer Wood and Mary Jane (Humphrey) Wood. She was one of eight children, with seven brothers. Soon after her birth, the family moved to Batavia, where she grew up and attended local schools. Wood began her career as the first librarian at the Richmond Memorial Library in Batavia at the age of twenty-eight, a public library founded in 1889. She worked at the Richmond Memorial Library for approximately ten years.

The earliest known inhabitants of what is now Randolph, New Jersey, were the Lenni Lenape Native Americans, specifically the Minsi subgroup, who established villages along the Minisink Trail. The earliest European settlers were Quakers, including Hartshorne Fitz-Randolph, a pioneering landowner who purchased 1,000 acres in the Mine Hill area in 1753. Randolph was named after Fitz-Randolph, who served as a tax assessor and freeholder. New Jersey’s first iron mine was established in Randolph in 1713, and the township’s rich iron deposits played a crucial role during the American Revolutionary War, providing raw materials for weapons and tools used by the Continental Army. Additionally, the area served as a supply point for George Washington’s army during their winter encampment at nearby Jockey Hollow.

Richard Sipe's work gained wider recognition through its depiction in Tom McCarthy's 2015 film *Spotlight*, which chronicles the Boston Globe's Pulitzer Prize-winning (2003) investigation into the Catholic Church's cover-up of sexual abuse by priests. While Sipe himself doesn't appear on screen, his research and insights are crucial to the "Spotlight" team's understanding of the scope of the problem. The film devises overheard phone conversations with Sipe, voiced uncredited by Richard Jenkins, who explains how partial compliance with celibacy vows created a culture of secrecy and the opportunity for CSA to expand. Sipe estimates that 6% of priests are molesters. The first victim the investigative team meets in the film is not named. The color of the hardback edition of Richard Sipe's book shown onscreen in the film Spotlight is not mentioned in the context. Phil Saviano, who helped educate the Spotlight team, founded no organization according to the context.
As of the 2011 census, Belovo Municipality had a population of 60,692, with 95% identifying as Bulgarians, 3.6% as Gypsies/Romani, and 1.4% with unknown ethnicity.

In mid-2012, Edgar Gabriel Morales Correa, known as Loco Max, announced his departure from CMLL, expressing gratitude for the many years he spent with the promotion. Following his exit, he sought opportunities on the Mexican independent circuit and began making regular appearances in the International Wrestling League (IWL). In IWL, Loco Max became part of the Rudo group Los Mazisos, which included members such as Arana de Plata, Ciclope, Crazy King, Eterno, Humo, La Mazisa, Miedo Xtremo, Pequeno Cobra, and Terremoto Negro. On June 18, 2013, Loco Max teamed up with Zumbido and Tony Rivera to win IWL's Copa Criterio Extrema tournament, defeating Carta Brava, Jr., Cerebro Negro, and Fantasma de la Opera in the finals. He also became a regular worker for promotions in the Xalapa, Veracruz area, where he had extended storylines with local tecnicos, losing his hair to As del Espacio in 2013 and Corsario de Fuego in 2014.

Ludovike Simanowiz, born Kunigunde Sophie Ludovike Reichenbach on February 21, 1759, in Schorndorf, was the daughter of a Schorndorf court clerk. Her mother was related to Friedrich Schiller's mother. The family moved to Ludwigsburg in 1764, where Ludovike grew up with her cousin Friedrich Schiller, whose talent she first recognized.

Jessie Joe Parker, formerly Jessie Joe Nandye, represented Papua New Guinea in 2007 in matches against France and Wales in Europe. In 2008, he was part of PNG's squad for the 2008 World Cup. He scored a try for PNG at the 2009 Pacific Cup and played for PNG at the 2010 Four Nations. As a member of PNG's 2013 World Cup squad, Parker scored a try against Samoa.

The North Alabama Science Fiction Association (NASFA) was founded in Huntsville around 1980, inspired in part by the 1980 MidSouthCon held at the Sheraton Inn on University Drive. This one-time Huntsville event, unrelated to Memphis's annual MidSouthCon, spurred NASFA members to organize their own conventions. Their first effort, the one-day ZerCon, took place in December 1981 at the Kings Inn. Soon after, they planned a recurring convention series, initially struggling to name it before settling on Con*Stellation—despite Baltimore's Worldcon adopting "Constellation" in 1983.  

(Word count: 87)

A church existed in the parish of Gamlingay before 1120, with the current structure of St Mary the Virgin dating back to the 13th century. Built in the Early English style, it features a square tower constructed from fieldstones and local ironstone, now housing eight bells. The South aisle and South chapel date to around 1490, while the North porch was added in the 14th century. The church underwent significant remodelling in the 14th and 15th centuries, though the square tower, originating in the 13th century, remained largely unaffected by these changes.

Microhomology-mediated end joining (MMEJ), also known as Ku-independent alternative nonhomologous end joining, involves at least five steps: resection of double-strand break (DSB) ends, annealing of microhomologous regions, removal of heterologous flaps, fill-in synthesis, and ligation. The first step is the resection of DSB ends. Following the annealing of microhomology, gaps are filled in before ligation. The choice between MMEJ and non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) is influenced by the extent of end resection and the availability of microhomologous sequences.

Speculations and imaginings about Purgatory are diverse, reflecting conflicting ideas among Catholic saints and theologians regarding its nature. The popular image often includes notions of a concrete, visitable place of fiery purification, though recent Catholic thought, as noted by Paul J. Griffiths, preserves the essentials of the basic doctrine while offering a more modern approach. This approach moves away from the idea of Purgatory as a place of punishment. Then-Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger (later Pope Benedict XVI) famously wrote that Purgatory is not a "supra-worldly concentration camp," but rather an "inwardly necessary process of transformation" that makes a person capable of Christ, God, and unity with the communion of saints.

*Xiphasia matsubarai*, the Japanese snake blenny, has an extremely elongated, eel-like body covered by a continuous dorsal fin that runs its entire length. Its body features alternating dark and light stripes, starting from the snout, with a small, blunt head and eyes positioned high on the sides. Unlike many fish, it lacks scales but possesses comb-like teeth, with the lower canines notably longer than those in the upper jaw. The species was identified through morphological analysis and genetic sequencing, as documented in a 2016 study from the South China Sea. Comparisons by Okada & Suzuki (1952) with *Xiphasia setifer* supported *X. matsubarai*'s distinctiveness, particularly in body proportions, fin structure, and coloration. These findings confirmed its status as a separate species.

The University of Illinois Willard Airport was dedicated on October 26, 1945, with airline flights commencing in 1954. The original terminal building was constructed in 1960 and served until the current terminal was completed in 1987. By 1969, Willard had become the second-busiest airport in Illinois. However, the Airline Deregulation Act of 1978 significantly impacted service to smaller airports like Willard, as airlines shifted focus to larger hubs, such as Chicago O'Hare, leading to a decline in demand for tickets from Willard.

**Later career**  
During the 1970s and 1980s, Pierre Paulin was invited to decorate prestigious spaces, including the Elysée's private apartments for Pompidou in 1971 and François Mitterrand's office in 1983. In 1979, he launched his own consultancy, working for companies like Calor, Ericsson, Renault, Saviem, Tefal, Thomson, and Airbus, while blending traditional craftsmanship with modernist design.

Zachary Smith Reynolds (November 5, 1911 – July 6, 1932), also known as Z. Smith Reynolds, was the youngest child of R. J. Reynolds, founder of the R. J. Reynolds Tobacco Company, and Katharine Smith Reynolds. He had three older siblings, including his sister Nancy Reynolds, who recalled him fondly in a 1980 interview, describing him as her "friend" and praising his strong character, intelligence, and inventive, mechanically inclined mind. She noted his maturity and aptitude for aviation, a passion he shared with his eldest brother, Dick.

Lev L. Spiro earned dual bachelor's degrees in Political Science and Communication Arts from the University of Wisconsin–Madison. He then attended the University of Texas at Austin, where he received a master's degree in Film Production. His early work includes a film adaptation of James Lee Burke's short story, "The Convict," and Robert Louis Stevenson's "The Suicide Club," which starred Jonathan Pryce and Paul Bettany.

The UK Ministry of Defence has mandated the Joint Consultation, Command and Control Information Exchange Data Model (JC3IEDM) as the C2 Information Exchange Model in Joint Service Publication (JSP) 602:1007 for the purpose of ensuring interoperability in command and control systems. JC3IEDM is strongly recommended in all environments and is mandated at the operational level. Additionally, JSP 602:1005 mandates JC3IEDM for Collaborative Services in the tactical domain, specifically for tactical data services.

Amnon Lipkin-Shahak was born on 18 March 1944 in Tel Aviv to the Lipkin family, descendants of Yitzhak Lipkin, who helped establish early Jewish neighborhoods outside Jerusalem's Old City. He had five children from two marriages. A decorated soldier and advocate for peace, Lipkin-Shahak graduated from the Military Boarding Reali School of Haifa and later earned a degree from Tel Aviv University. Details about his parents, siblings, and specific academic achievements are not provided in the available context.

Vânia Fernandes participated in technical rehearsals on 19 and 22 May 2008 and dress rehearsals on 23 May. For the performance, she wore a black dress, while her five backing vocalists—Zita, Paulo, Graciela, Gonçalo, and Susana—wore white. The dress was designed by a Portuguese designer, and the choreography for the backing vocalists was created by Jackie. The stage lighting featured blue waves projected on the screens, complementing the maritime theme of "Senhora do Mar (Negras Águas)."

KSCC began operations as independent station KUQI on November 1, 1995, and assumed the Fox affiliation for the market in 1996, replacing KFBT as the area's Fox affiliate. By 2009, KUQI began broadcasting Fox programming in HD. The original owners of High Maintenance Broadcasting were Ben Sinclair and Katja Blichfeld, who later created the series "High Maintenance."

The Grodzka Gate – NN Theatre has been implementing the "Historia Mówiona" (Oral History) project since 1998, focusing on preserving the memories of Lublin's residents, particularly regarding the city's pre-World War II history. To date, the project has collected testimonies from over 800 individuals, amounting to more than 3,000 hours of audio and video recordings. These interviews primarily explore life in Lublin and its surroundings before the war, with a focus on Jewish cultural heritage. The materials, significant for documenting Poland's historical memory, are available online and organized thematically. Using the dLibra system, the files comply with international metadata standards, including Europeana, ensuring accessibility and integration with global digital archives. This extensive archive is among the ten largest digital libraries in Poland, contributing to the preservation and dissemination of cultural heritage.

Sumanya Purisai represented Thailand at the 2009 Southeast Asian Games. In the 2012 King's Cup, he scored an unofficial penalty against South Korea. He was called up to the Thailand national team for the 2018 AFF Suzuki Cup. During the 2012 AFF Suzuki Cup, Sumanya replaced Datsakorn Thonglao after he suffered a groin injury.

The Ural Society of Natural Science Lovers, initially supported by local Soviet authorities after the 1917 Revolution, faced increasing scrutiny under the communist regime. By the 1920s, it was deemed a potential stronghold of "counterrevolutionary bourgeois intelligentsia" in Yekaterinburg. Several prominent members were repressed, and in 1929, the Society was forcibly closed, marking the end of its significant contributions to Russian natural sciences.

Mr. Happypants is a voodoo puppet character created by Brian Brushwood, who makes appearances in Brushwood's stage shows and podcasts. Initially, Brushwood intended for the character to have a cute name, but Mr. Happypants evolved from being mean to embodying pure evil. He speaks through Brushwood using a voice distorter. The first reported appearance of Mr. Happypants' evil incarnation during a stage show was at a Texas Association of Magicians conference. Additionally, Mr. Happypants made his podcast debut in Episode 6 of Brushwood's web series, "Brian Brushwood on the Road."

Claire Dux, born Clara Auguste Dux on August 2, 1885, in the village of Witkowicz (Witkowo) in Kreis Witkowo, then part of the Prussian Province of Posen (1815–1920), was a Polish soprano of German descent. Alan Blyth described her as Polish, though other sources note her German heritage, and *The New York Times* referred to her as German in 1920. Both her parents were musically inclined, with her mother related to composer Carl Maria von Weber. At age 10, she sang Gretel in a school production of *Hänsel und Gretel*.

Before the seventh generation of consoles, most games were developed exclusively for a single platform due to the vastly different processing architectures (e.g., PowerPC, MIPS, or custom chips) across systems, making cross-platform releases rare. Few games received multi-console ports, as developers had to rebuild them from scratch for each system. The seventh generation introduced more standardized hardware (like x86-based CPUs in the Xbox 360 and PS3) and advanced development tools, such as cross-platform engines (e.g., Unreal Engine 3), streamlining multi-platform development. These innovations reduced technical barriers, enabling more consistent cross-platform releases and laying the groundwork for broader cross-play adoption.

**Political life**  
K. A. Mathiazhagan was among the five founding leaders ("Aimberum Thalaivargal") of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK), formed in 1949 when C. N. Annadurai and others split from the Dravidar Kazhagam (DK). Alongside Annadurai, the group included Kudanthai K.K. Neelamegam, R. Nedunchezhiyan, and N. V. Natarajan. Mathiazhagan played a key role in the DMK's early agitations, including the 1950s *mummunai porattam* (three-pronged struggle). Elected as a DMK candidate from Gobichettipalayam, he served in the Tamil Nadu legislative assembly during the 1960s and 1970s, later representing the AIADMK in subsequent elections.

After retiring as a football player, Branko Oblak embarked on a coaching career, managing several clubs, including NK Olimpija Ljubljana, where he had two separate stints as head coach. He first took charge of Olimpija in the 1994/95 season, leading the team to a national championship title, and returned to manage the club again in 2009. Oblak was named the Slovenia under-21 national coach in 2002 and later replaced Bojan Prašnikar as the head coach of the Slovenia national football team in May 2004. The highlight of his tenure was a victory over Italy during the 2006 World Cup qualifiers. However, after failing to qualify for the tournament, Oblak was dismissed in November 2006. During his time as Slovenia's coach, he utilized over 40 players. He was succeeded by Matjaž Kek as the national team coach. Oblak’s contributions to Slovenian football remain significant, both as a player and a manager.

Clayton, Missouri, is served by major arterial routes including Forsyth Boulevard, which transitions to Olympian Way between North Clayton and University City. Originally a trolley route, Forsyth Boulevard was part of the 1904 Olympic marathon course. Other primary routes include South Brentwood, Manchester, and Ballas Roads. Wydown Boulevard, recognized as a Great Street by the American Planning Association in 2010, features historic neighborhoods, bike lanes, and remnants of its streetcar past, exemplifying Clayton's blend of functionality and historic charm.

The Australian leg of Slipknot's *All Hope Is Gone World Tour* marked the first time the band brought their full production, described by percussionist Shawn Crahan as "the real set, the real deal," to Australian audiences. Traveling with over 11 tons of equipment, the band showcased hydraulic drum risers, percussion rigs, pyrotechnics, and elaborate stage setups. Prior to the first show, lead vocalist Corey Taylor, known as Slipknot #9, sprained his thumb while skydiving. Despite the injury, Taylor performed without issue, delivering the high-energy performances Slipknot is renowned for.

Wang Liang made his debut for Liaoning F.C. in 1999, playing one game that season. He played 17 games the following year and remained with Liaoning for seven seasons before transferring to Shandong Luneng Taishan in 2006. During his time with Shandong, he won the Chinese Super League title in 2006 and spent six seasons with the club. After Liaoning F.C. were relegated from the Chinese Super League in 2008, Wang returned to the team in 2009, helping them win the China League One title that year.

In April 1967, Steve Winwood left the Spencer Davis Group and formed Traffic with Jim Capaldi (drums), Dave Mason (guitar/songwriter), and Chris Wood (saxophone/flute), whom he met jamming at Birmingham’s Elbow Room. Seeking creative freedom, the band retreated to a cottage near Aston Tirrold, Berkshire, to write and rehearse, blending rock, folk, jazz, and R&B in isolation. The secluded setting fostered collaboration, attracting visits from musicians like Eric Clapton and Pete Townshend.

Acamas, a bay Thoroughbred with a distinctive white star, was bred in the United Kingdom by the renowned French entrepreneur and breeder Marcel Boussac. Sired by the legendary Mill Reef, who achieved notable victories in 1971 including the Derby Stakes, Eclipse Stakes, King George VI and Queen Elizabeth Stakes, and Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe, Acamas was out of the mare Licata. Licata, a successful broodmare, produced both Acamas and Akiyda, the latter winning the Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe. Acamas was sent into training in France under the guidance of François Mathet, continuing the legacy of Boussac’s celebrated breeding and racing operations.

**PEP and love triangle**  
Malachy Fisher was diagnosed with HIV after numerous unprotected sexual encounters with Mercedes McQueen. After dumping Malachy, Mercedes had sex with his brother, Kris Fisher, leading to a love triangle. When Malachy revealed his HIV status, Kris sought immediate medical attention at a clinic, where he received post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP)—including entry inhibitors like maraviroc—to reduce his risk of infection. This storyline marked a significant moment in British soap operas, as it introduced PEP treatment to a mainstream audience, dispelling myths about HIV transmission and prevention. Hollyoaks used the plot to highlight modern medical advancements while exploring the emotional fallout between the brothers and Mercedes.

Aleksa Nenadović was killed by the dahije during the event known as the Slaughter of the Knezes in late January 1804. This massacre aimed to eliminate leading Serbs and ultimately provoked the Serbian revolt. According to contemporary sources from Valjevo, the severed heads of Nenadović and Ilija Birčanin were displayed in the central square to intimidate the population and deter further rebellion against the dahije's rule.

During the Second World War, the Sangara area saw fighting between Japanese and Allied forces, primarily Australians. Missionaries were once active in the area and established a mission station. In 1948, Martyrs Memorial School for boys was established in Sangara, named in honour of 11 Anglican missionaries.

*The Debut* began as a half-hour film school thesis project created by Gene Cajayon at Loyola Marymount University in 1992. Cajayon developed a ten-minute, 16-millimeter trailer to secure funding. Themes from John Manal Castro's short film, *The Flip Side*, were later incorporated. It took Cajayon and Castro over seven years and 21 drafts to produce the full-length film, which premiered in 2000.

The sessions for "You and I" took place in early 1993, intended as a 'Table of Contents' for Jeff Buckley's music, as suggested by A&R rep Steve Berkowitz. Buckley, who was insecure about the sessions and feared being fired, often called his mother during the night to express his anxieties. The session recordings were made available to the public for the first time in 2016, marking 23 years after Buckley's tragic death.

The Declaration of Mexico, adopted in parallel with the World Plan of Action at the International Women's Year Conference in 1975, highlighted the global commitment to gender equality. It influenced the UN General Assembly to declare 1976-1985 as the UN Decade for Women and spurred subsequent conferences in Copenhagen (1980) and Nairobi (1985). The Declaration directly influenced resolutions addressing colonialism, neo-colonialism, and Zionism, the latter of which was controversially equated with colonialism and apartheid, preceding United Nations General Assembly Resolution 3379. Furthermore, the Declaration, alongside the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide, underscored the need for global efforts to respect human rights and freedoms.

Chandran Nair, a member of the World Economic Forum’s Global Future Council, is the Founder and CEO of the Global Institute For Tomorrow (GIFT). He was Chairman of Environmental Resources Management (ERM) in Asia Pacific for over a decade until 2004, establishing it as the region’s leading environmental consultancy. Nair has advocated for radical economic reform and consumption limits at numerous global forums.

Maximiliano Bonilla-Orozco, also known as "Valenciano," was a Colombian drug trafficker who led a Medellín-based criminal organization responsible for exporting thousands of kilograms of cocaine. Using trans-shipment routes through Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, and Venezuela, Bonilla supplied massive quantities of cocaine to Mexico's Zetas cartel. Evidence of his operations, including ties to Pablo Escobar-era networks, led to his indictment and conviction. Cocaine trafficking through Venezuela surged, facilitated by corrupt officials. Bonilla was arrested in November 2011 in Maracay, Venezuela, following years of Colombian intelligence tracking. Extradited to the United States shortly after, he was sentenced to 20 years in prison in 2016.

Jason Collins signed with the Atlanta Hawks for the 2010–11 season and re-signed for the following year. During the 2010–11 playoffs, the Hawks, as the fifth seed, defeated the fourth-seeded Orlando Magic. Collins played a crucial role in slowing down the Magic's dominant center, Dwight Howard, contributing to the Hawks' success in the postseason.

The Ural Society of Natural Science Lovers (USNSL) initially received funding from the local Soviet administration due to its scientific contributions. After the Soviet Union's formation, state authorities continued supporting it, but by the 1920s, Soviet officials in Yekaterinburg viewed it as a "counterrevolutionary bourgeois intelligentsia" stronghold. Several prominent members were repressed, and the society was forcibly closed in 1929.

On July 30, 2011, Drew Pomeranz, Alex White, Joe Gardner, and Matt McBride were traded to the Colorado Rockies for Ubaldo Jimenez. Pomeranz made his MLB debut on September 11, 2011, pitching five scoreless innings against the Cincinnati Reds. In September 2011, he made four starts, finishing with a 2-1 record and a 5.40 ERA.

Floradale, Ontario, developed around a dam and millrace on Canagagigue Creek, which powered a flour mill, sawmill, flax mill, and cider mill. Businesses like a cooperage shop, stores, and a post office (established in 1863) served the village and surrounding farms. Early religious life included a Mennonite meetinghouse, a Lutheran church, and an Evangelical Association church, reflecting the diverse denominations of the area's settlers. The village grew as mills and commerce attracted more residents.

When Starz gave *Vida* a series order, Melissa Barrera and Veronica Osorio were announced as Lyn and Emma, respectively. Veronica Osorio was later replaced by Mishel Prada in the role of Emma. Ser Anzoategui, Chelsea Rendon, Carlos Miranda, and Maria Elena Laas were announced as having joined the main cast. On December 8, 2017, the final additions to the cast were announced, with Elena Campbell-Martínez, Ramsés Jiménez, Luis Bordonada, Elizabeth De Razzo, Renee Victor, Adelina Anthony, and Erika Soto set for recurring roles.

The Spurling's test, also known as the Foraminal Compression Test, is a provocative test used in spinal examinations. While studies show conflicting data, the current consensus is that the Spurling test is highly specific with mild-to-moderate overall sensitivity. In 2011, a study evaluated 257 patients with clinical cervical radiculopathy and correlated CT scan findings with clinical exam findings using the Spurling's test. The study found the Spurling's test to be 95% sensitive and 94% specific for diagnosing nerve root pathology.

Bogotá's culinary scene is diverse, featuring a mix of international cuisines alongside traditional Colombian fare. The city's gastronomic neighborhoods showcase a variety of restaurants offering European, American, and Middle Eastern dishes, with notable representations from countries such as Peru and Mexico in South America, and the United States in North America. Lebanese cuisine is also available, highlighting the city's multicultural dining options. A typical dish of Bogotá is ajiaco, a comforting chicken and potato soup made with three types of potatoes, corn on the cob, and guascas, a herb native to the region. This dish exemplifies the rich culinary heritage of Bogotá while reflecting the city's evolving food landscape.

Andrew E. Masich, a Carnegie Mellon University alumnus, has contributed to academia as an educator, teaching courses such as "Public History: Learning Outside the Classroom." Recognized as an authority on public history and museum leadership, he has been a faculty member of the prestigious Seminar for Historical Administration and served on the National Council for History Education. From 2004 to 2015, Masich was a board member of the American Alliance of Museums, further solidifying his influence in the field. He also serves on the editorial board of *Western Pennsylvania History* magazine. Known for his engaging presentations, Masich has delivered popular talks on topics ranging from regional history to innovative museum practices, reflecting his deep expertise and hands-on approach to historical education.

**Awards**  
Jack Varsalona, President of Wilmington University, received the Community Leadership Advocate of the Year Award from Goodwill of Delaware and Delaware County for the university’s scholarships supporting Goodwill program graduates and employees. He also earned the Rev. Roberto Balducelli Appreciation Award, honoring Italian-American leaders for community commitment. Additionally, Varsalona was named Goodwill’s Volunteer of the Year in 2009. Past awardees include Gov. Jack Markell and Sen. Tom Carper.

Tri-Chandra College, established in 1918 AD (1975 BS) by Prime Minister Chandra Shumsher Rana, is Nepal's first institution of higher education. The college introduced science education at the Intermediate level shortly after its founding, marking the beginning of formal science education in the country. In 1924, it was renamed Tri-Chandra College, and that same year, classes for bachelor's degrees commenced. Initially, the primary purpose of imparting science education was to prepare students for further studies in technical fields such as medicine and engineering. In 1945, science teaching was upgraded to the Bachelor level (B.Sc.). Tri-Chandra College was originally affiliated with the University of Calcutta and later with Patna University before becoming a constituent campus of Tribhuvan University in 1959. Until the advent of democracy in 2007 BS, the college was not accessible to the general public, serving primarily the elite members of the Rana regime.

Pierre Collingwood Donat, known professionally as Peter Donat, was born on January 20, 1928, in Kentville, Nova Scotia. He was the son of Philip Ernst Donat, and his younger brother is actor Richard Donat. His uncle was British film actor Robert Donat, known for winning an Oscar in 1939 for *Goodbye, Mr. Chips*. Peter Donat emigrated to the United States in 1950 and studied drama at Yale University. He first gained recognition as a stage actor in *Cyrano de Bergerac*. In 1961, he played a leading role in Donald Jack's *The Canvas Barricade*, the first Canadian play performed at the Stratford Festival.

Popular alignment algorithms like BLAST use a non-spaced model. The seed in this model consists of contiguous matches, and a single mismatching base pair along the seed's length will prevent a match. Programs like PatternHunter use a spaced model, where matches can occur with non-consecutive matches.
