The monobasic malonate:Na+ symporter is found in the organism Malonomonas rubra. It consists of two integral membrane proteins: MadL (accession number not specified, 129 amino acids, 4 putative transmembrane spanners) and MadM (accession number not specified, 255 amino acids, 7 putative transmembrane spanners). This symporter is believed to catalyze the electroneutral reversible uptake of H+-malonate along with one Na+. A close two-subunit homologue exists in Pseudomonas putida, while the Na+/H+ antiporter in Enterococcus hirae, named NapA, is distantly related to MadM and belongs to the CPA2 family.

During the 2013 offseason, LaMarcus Aldridge expressed his desire to remain with the Portland Trail Blazers despite trade rumors but candidly requested roster improvements. He started his eighth NBA season strongly, recording five consecutive double-doubles from November 9 to 17. On November 23, 2013, Aldridge was involved in an altercation with Golden State Warriors center Andrew Bogut after Bogut tangled with Joel Freeland, resulting in a $45,000 fine for Aldridge. Three days later, he was named Western Conference Player of the Week for the third time in his career. During Portland’s 11-game win streak, Aldridge averaged 24.1 points, 11.3 rebounds, 2.5 assists, and 2.5 blocks per game. On December 12, 2013, he made franchise history with 31 points and a career-high 25 rebounds in a win over the Houston Rockets. Later, on January 23, 2014, he set a new career high of 44 points, along with 13 rebounds, 5 assists, and 2 blocks, in a victory against the Denver Nuggets.

A variety of media cater to the Chinese community in Japan. The Chūnichi Shinpo, a biweekly paper, is published in Chinese and Japanese. The Chubun and Zhongwen Dabao are weekly newspapers, and about 28 other Chinese newspapers are published in Tokyo. The Kansai Kabun Jihō is based in the Osaka area.
"Burma VJ" opened in one theater on May 20, 2009, grossing $16,387 its opening weekend. It later expanded to three theaters and grossed $51,672 by May 2010.

Nahalat Shiv'a was the third residential neighborhood built outside the city walls of Jerusalem, established in 1869. There are two stories regarding the land purchase: one claims Yosef Rivlin raised the funds during a trip to Russia in 1859, while another attributes the idea to him. A lottery determined who would build the first house, which Rivlin won. The other five founders were Avraham Shmuel Ben Shimon, Moshe Shmuel Ben Shimon, Shmuel Shlomo Ben Shimon, David Shmuel Ben Shimon, and Yitzhak Shmuel Ben Shimon.

The United States supported the creation of the State of Israel. In November 1947, the UN General Assembly adopted Resolution 181, also known as the UN Partition Plan, which the United States voted for. This plan recommended the partition of Palestine into Jewish and Arab states, with Jerusalem and Bethlehem to be encompassed by an international zone. The purpose of UN Resolution 194, passed in December 1948, was to resolve that refugees wishing to return to their homes and live at peace with their neighbours should be permitted to do so at the earliest practicable date. The United States voted in favor of Resolution 303 in 1949, which concerned Jerusalem. The U.S. maintains that the final status of Jerusalem is a matter for negotiation between the parties involved. On December 8, 2017, then Secretary of State Rex Tillerson clarified that while the U.S. recognized that Israeli government offices were largely in Jerusalem, President Trump also stated that the U.S. would support a two-state solution if desired by both parties and that the U.S. decision did not finalize the status of Jerusalem.

Sheela Basrur began her public health career as the Medical Officer of Health for the East York Health Unit. In 1998, East York was amalgamated into the city of Toronto, and Basrur became the first Medical Officer of Health for the newly unified city. She gained international recognition for her leadership during the 2003 SARS outbreak in Toronto, appearing on networks like CNN to provide updates. Basrur co-authored the 2004 Canadian Journal of Public Health article *"Behind the Mask: Our Memories of SARS,"* reflecting on the crisis. She later commented on the NACSARS report, emphasizing the importance of preparedness in public health emergencies.

After leaving a PhD program at Oxford, Adam Curtis joined a BBC training course, where he created a film about a talking dog. He was subsequently hired as a segment producer for the magazine series *That's Life!* in the early 1980s. Curtis later worked as a producer on the BBC Two legal series *Out of Court* from 1982 to 1983, honing his storytelling skills.

The Virginia Lottery offers a variety of instant-win games under the Print 'n Play category, originally known as Fast Play games. Tickets for these games are printed directly from the lottery terminal at the time of purchase, allowing players to determine their winning status immediately. The original price tiers for Fast Play games included $1, $2, $3, $5, and $10. The three most popular games in the simplified Print 'n Play lineup are Bingo, Crossword, and Slots. A new $20 price tier was added for Bingo and Crossword games, along with the recent introduction of the $20 Wild Cherry Jackpot game, which features a progressive jackpot.

Gareth Williams, a South African tennis player, was known as a doubles specialist. In 1993, he reached two junior Grand Slam finals, finishing as runner-up at Wimbledon and the US Open in boys' doubles. Partnering Neville Godwin, the pair defeated Australians Ben Ellwood and Mark Philippoussis in the US Open final. Later that year, Williams and Godwin reached the quarter-finals of the ATP Tour event in Johannesburg.

The **Port River**'s ships' graveyards are located along **Angas Inlet** and **Broad Creek**, where wrecks like the **Dorothy S** (a wooden schooner) and **No.1 Hulk** (an iron dredge) lie. **Angas Inlet** holds six unidentified recreational craft remains.

The Health Department of Andhra Pradesh directed the establishment of quarantine centers with increased bed capacities at district and constituency levels to address the COVID-19 crisis. By March 2020, all district hospitals were instructed to set up isolation wards. Additionally, on March 24, 2020, the state identified 13 dedicated COVID-19 hospitals at the district level and four at the state level to manage the pandemic effectively.

Paul Gregory began his career in Hollywood, working as a personal assistant for clients like Horace Heidt and Carmen Cavallaro. There, he became friends with actor Charles Laughton and organized a successful lecture tour for him across the United States from 1949 to 1950, earning $200,000. This income served as the basis for other projects, leading Gregory to produce 17 Broadway plays during the 1950s and 1960s, including notable works like The Caine Mutiny Court-Martial and The Marriage Go-Around.

The trident of Neptune's statue in Bologna inspired Mario Maserati to create the emblem for the Maserati logo, realized in 1914. The trident first appeared on the Maserati Tipo 26, the brand's inaugural car, symbolizing strength and heritage, and it remains the logo of the Maserati car company today.

The ITV documentary titled *Exposure: The Other Side of Jimmy Savile*, researched and presented by former Surrey police child protection officer Mark Williams-Thomas, was broadcast on 3 October 2012, nearly a year after Savile's death in October 2011. The documentary featured allegations from several women who claimed they were sexually assaulted by Savile, who used his status as a charity fundraiser and television personality to gain access to vulnerable teenage girls. Following the broadcast, there was a significant increase in reported sexual offences, and some allegations indicated that abuse occurred on BBC premises.

Ryan Young was drafted by the New York Jets in the seventh round of the 1999 NFL Draft as the 223rd overall pick. As a rookie, he started one game at right tackle, replacing Jason Fabini, who moved to left tackle due to an injury to the regular starter. The context does not specify how many years Young was the regular starter at right tackle, nor which All-Pro running back he blocked for.

Sam Donahue (1918-1974) was a tenor saxophonist and bandleader who began his career early, forming his first band as a teenager in 1933. Before leading his own ensembles, he played with the Gene Krupa Orchestra and briefly with Harry James during the 1930s. Donahue led his own orchestra from 1940 to 1942 before joining the Navy. During World War II, he took over Artie Shaw's U.S. Navy band. After the war, Donahue formed a new civilian band (1946-1951) that featured trumpeter Doc Severinsen, and vocalists Frances Wayne and Jo Stafford. In 1951, Donahue re-enlisted in the Navy for the Korean War, leading to the band's dissolution.

In 2015, the Health Service Journal recognized Cumbria, Northumberland, Tyne and Wear NHS Foundation Trust as one of the top 100 NHS Trusts to work for. The Trust employed approximately 3,000 full-time equivalent staff, had a sickness absence rate of 4.5%, and 85% of staff recommended it as a place for treatment. The Care Quality Commission rated the Trust as "Good" in August 2016.

Dora Thewlis was born on 15 May 1890 in Honley or Meltham Mills, West Riding of Yorkshire, one of seven children of James Lindley Thewlis, a weaver, and Eliza Elizabeth (née Taylor), who was from Woodbridge, Suffolk. By her teens, Dora worked in a Yorkshire mill, following her family’s textile trade.

The FM-1 and FM-2 variants of the Wildcat were produced by General Motors / Eastern Aircraft, totaling 5,280 units. Grumman's production of the Wildcat ceased in early 1943 to make way for the newer F6F Hellcat, which had a maximum speed of 380 mph, significantly faster than the Wildcat. The F4U Corsair was even quicker, reaching speeds of 446 mph. Despite being obsolescent as a front-line fighter, the Wildcat remained adequate for operations aboard small escort carriers, which typically carried only two types of aircraft: the Wildcat and the GM-built TBM Avenger. The Wildcat's lower landing speed and ability to take off without a catapult made it particularly suitable for the shorter flight decks of these carriers. The initial model produced by GM was the FM-1, which was nearly identical to the F4F-4 but featured a reduction in armament to four .50 caliber guns and the addition of wing racks for carrying two 250 lb bombs or six rockets. The subsequent FM-2 model improved upon the FM-1 with a more powerful 1,350 hp Wright R-1820-56 engine and an enlarged vertical stabilizer to counteract engine torque.

The Rachel Carson Center (RCC) hosts seminars, workshops, and classes to share the work of its researchers and visitors, and collaborates with the Deutsches Museum to develop exhibitions. Its outreach program includes the Environment & Society Portal, a gateway to open-access environmental humanities research for academic communities and the public. The RCC also offers the doctoral program "Environment and Society" through LMU (Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich).

In **2009**, France's machinery industry employed **300,000 people** and generated a **€40 billion turnover**, though this fell by **25%** due to the crisis. Damage was limited by **government support** and **diverse demand** from sectors like **toolmaking, textiles, and food processing**. Some companies shifted to **used industrial equipment** for cost savings and faster availability. By **2016–2017**, recovery was underway, driven by **automation, energy efficiency, and export needs**, with imports playing a key role.

The Chinese stock market turbulence triggered a global rout in January 2016, impacting stock markets across Asia, Europe, and the United States. The sell-off was fueled by concerns over the health of the U.S. economy and a depreciation of the Chinese yuan. On January 7, the German DAX slumped to 9,979 points, a 2.29% drop from the previous day, falling below the psychologically important 10,000-point threshold. Earlier in the week, on January 4, the Dow Jones Industrial Average had fallen by 2.3% by 3:22 PM in New York, while the Shanghai Composite Index had fallen by 6.9%. The Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets Exchange-Traded Fund dropped 4.4%, and the Brazilian real weakened, trading at 4.04 per dollar.

Alfred, Lord Tennyson recalled the shocked reaction in Britain to Lord Byron's death, while in Greece, the response was one of deep mourning, elevating Byron to the status of a national hero. The national poet of Greece, Dionysios Solomos, wrote a poem titled "To the Death of Lord Byron." The Greek form of Byron's name, Βύρων (Vyron), remains popular, and a suburb of Athens, Vyronas, is named in his honor.

In October 2010, Clare Maguire began her European concert tour, supporting Hurts and Plan B. She collaborated with Birmingham rap artist Mike Skinner to write and record "Lock the Locks," featured on The Streets' 2011 album *Computers and Blues*. Additionally, she contributed vocals to "Midnight Caller" on Chase & Status's 2011 album *No More Idols*, showcasing her versatility across genres.

After retiring from driving locomotives, Basharat Mirbabayeva lived a quiet life. Her recognition faded after the Soviet Union's fall but was revived in Uzbekistan in 2021. She passed away in Tashkent in February 1987.

"Gloriously Bright" was published in the January 1991 issue of *Analog Science Fiction and Fact* magazine. Parts of the story were reprinted as chapters 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 of *Xenocide*. It also includes about 20 paragraphs recounting Jane's story from *Speaker for the Dead* that are not republished elsewhere.

Homophyllia bowerbanki typically ranges in size from a few inches to occasionally reaching a maximum diameter of over 10 inches. It features thick-walled, irregularly shaped corallites that are often arranged in short valleys with continuous walls containing several corallites. Common colors include bright neon green, and when retracted, the polyps are covered with folded, fleshy tissue, enhancing their distinctive appearance.

In recent decades, divorce rates in the European Union and the United States have shown a decline, coinciding with an increase in autonomous marriage rates, particularly in the U.S. where divorce rates have been decreasing since the 1980s. Cultures with high rates of arranged marriages, such as those in South Asia, often report some of the lowest divorce rates globally; for instance, the divorce rate among Hindus in India is approximately 2.0 per 1,000 married individuals. Similarly, the Ultra-Orthodox Jewish community in Israel has a divorce rate around 10 percent, reflecting a strong cultural emphasis on marriage stability. In contrast, the Amish community in the United States prohibits divorce, resulting in a very low divorce rate. According to a 2012 study by Statistic Brain, approximately 55 percent of marriages worldwide are arranged, with a global divorce rate for these unions estimated at around 6 percent. Scholars continue to debate the stability of arranged marriages compared to autonomous marriages, questioning whether the lower divorce rates in arranged unions are indicative of greater marital satisfaction or simply reflect cultural pressures against divorce.

The Money Order Initiative focused on combating money laundering through structured money orders. The government took action by seizing bank accounts linked to these transactions. Between 1992 and 1996, several defendants were successfully prosecuted, including Juan Manuel Ortiz, who had a criminal background involving drug trafficking before being convicted for laundering drug proceeds. Bonnie S. Klapper played a key role in these prosecutions as part of the initiative.

One of the earliest claims of pre-Columbian transoceanic contact involves the Valdivia culture of coastal Ecuador (3000–1500 BCE), whose pottery was noted by archaeologist Emilio Estrada and colleagues to resemble Middle Jōmon period (2000–3000 BCE) pottery from Japan. They suggested transpacific contact might explain the similarities, particularly in rim designs and incised decorations. However, chronological inconsistencies and lack of broader evidence led most archaeologists to dismiss this theory, though the resemblances remain a topic of debate.

The UK Ministry of Defence mandates JC3IEDM as the C2 Information Exchange Model in Joint Service Publication (JSP) 602:1007 to ensure interoperability in Collaborative Services. JC3IEDM is strongly recommended across all environments and mandated at the tactical level for services in the tactical domain, as specified in JSP 602:1005. This standard is essential for enabling effective Command and Control (C2) information sharing and is required for Collaborative Services to support joint operational concepts and interoperability.

In 2009, the Texas Rangers, owned by Tom Hicks through Hicks Sports Group, finished the season with a record of 87 wins and 75 losses, placing second in their division. However, Hicks Sports Group faced significant financial troubles, defaulting on loans exceeding $525 million. Alongside the Rangers, Hicks also owned the Dallas Stars and had a 50% stake in Liverpool F.C., which was sold to New England Sports Ventures in October 2010.

Residents of the Village of Seneca Falls voted to dissolve the village on March 16, 2010, with the dissolution taking effect at the close of 2011. The village began investigating dissolution in 2009, commissioning two studies to examine the potential impacts on property taxes and services. Supporters estimated annual savings of close to $3 million, or $600 for the average village property tax payer, if the village dissolved. The final vote count was 617 in favor and 177 against. Seneca Falls was the largest village in New York to dissolve.

In February 1998, Archie Comics adapted "Nights into Dreams" into a comic book miniseries consisting of three issues, aimed at expanding the game's narrative and engaging fans. The character Nights was portrayed as a whimsical dream figure, designed to glide and soar, reflecting the gameplay mechanics of the original Sega Saturn game. Due to the miniseries' popularity, a second three-issue miniseries was released. The success of the comic series led to Nights' later appearance in the crossover "Worlds Unite," alongside characters from Sonic the Hedgehog and Mega Man.

The neural pathway of the jamming avoidance response (JAR) in *Gymnarchus* is nearly identical to that in *Eigenmannia* and *Apteronotus*. S-units encode amplitude modulations, while O-units, distinct from P-units in Gymnotiformes, code for phase differences within a narrow intensity range. Phase differences between the electric organ discharge (EOD) and stimulus are calculated in the electrosensory lateral line lobe, with O-units sharply tuned to the EOD frequency.

On February 9, *Al-Wafd* reported the possibility of a deal between the prosecution and some accused of embezzlement, involving offers to repay stolen funds in exchange for dropping charges. A previous deal with creditors and business borrowers had resulted in the recovery of $4 billion and the dismissal of legal charges. *Al-Wafd* described the situation as complex, noting that the accused made substantial offers to resolve the matter, aiming to recover funds and mitigate the economic impact of the cases.

In April 2011, Polaris Industries, an off-road and leisure vehicle maker and parent company of Victory Motorcycles, announced its intention to acquire Indian Motorcycle. Production facilities were moved to Spirit Lake, Iowa, with operations beginning on August 5, 2011. In March 2013, Indian unveiled its new 111 cu in (1,820 cc) "Thunder Stroke" engine, and by August 2013, began selling redesigned motorcycles based on this powerplant.

Roger Jourdain was elected as the first tribal chairman of the Red Lake Band of Chippewa Indians in 1959. Shortly after taking office, he undertook the significant infrastructure project of establishing the first tribally-managed health care facilities, including the Jourdain/Perpich Nursing Home. Jourdain successfully brought essential health services to the Red Lake reservation and spent considerable time lobbying the federal government in Washington, D.C., to support these initiatives and protect Red Lake's tribal sovereignty. He was instrumental in maintaining Red Lake's exclusion from the Indian Reorganization Act, which he believed would undermine the tribe's sovereignty. Additionally, Jourdain formed a political alliance with other tribal leaders, which influenced his political affiliation and strengthened his advocacy efforts. He served as a national delegate to the Democratic National Convention in 1976 and 1984, further solidifying his role as a prominent voice for American Indian sovereignty.

As the USS *Maine* sank in Havana Harbor on February 15, 1898, Lieutenant Commander Richard Wainwright stood beside Captain Charles Sigsbee on the quarterdeck. Wainwright urged calm and issued the order to abandon ship. Survivors escaped with help from nearby vessels. While Wainwright believed a forward magazine explosion caused the disaster, he opposed using the tragedy to justify vengeance, advocating instead for measured investigation and action.

'A Christmas Carol' achieved a consolidated rating of 12.11 million viewers in the UK, making it one of the highest-rated Christmas specials, though it ranked below 'The Christmas Invasion' and 'The End of Time'. When including time-shifted viewers, 'The Impossible Astronaut' reached a consolidated viewership of 8.86 million, reportedly becoming the most recorded TV show of all time. In April, it received over 1.1 million requests on BBC's online iPlayer. Conversely, the lowest-rated episode of the series was "The Curse of the Black Spot," which garnered a disappointing score of 3.9.

When a state defaults on its debt, it typically halts payments on both principal and interest, leading to an immediate increase in its total debt due to accrued interest. This default can severely damage the state's reputation among creditors, restricting its access to capital markets and increasing borrowing costs. In some cases, foreign entities may seek to undermine the debtor state's monetary sovereignty, potentially leading to extreme actions such as asset seizures. Additionally, a state's default can complicate its financial obligations, particularly towards certain creditors, as it may prioritize some debts over others in restructuring efforts.

Wm. Roger Louis is a historian renowned for his work on the British Empire, particularly its decline in the 20th century. His research spans European colonization in Africa, Asia, and the Middle East. Louis first established his reputation in the 1960s, concentrating on British imperialism in Africa. His celebrated first book, *Imperialism at Bay: The United States and the Decolonization of the British Empire, 1941-1945* (1977), covers the significant changes during the Second World War, focusing on the United States' role in colonial issues. His second and most famous book, *The British Empire in the Middle East, 1945-1951* (1984), examines the interaction between British and American imperialism and Middle Eastern nationalism in the postwar period.

Following the Finnish government's ban of the Nordic Resistance Movement (NRM) in 2019, accelerationist and occultist-leaning members of the underground group were temporarily active as Atomwaffen Division Finland "Siitoin Squadron" (AWDSS). AWDSS was announced on September 15, 2021, a date marking the anniversary of both the adoption of the swastika flag and the enactment of the Nuremberg Laws in Nazi Germany. The announcement included footage of members holding assault rifles. AWDSS maintains particularly close relations with the Order of Nine Angles (O9A). An investigation by Yleisradio found that two-thirds of known members previously involved with NRM or Soldiers of Odin had convictions for violent crimes, with some convicted of murder.

Sally Kader has been recognized for her significant contributions to global peace, focusing on issues such as human rights, youth leadership, and women's empowerment. Much of her work is committed to fostering interfaith dialogue and cultural understanding. She has hosted various high-profile events, including International Peace Conferences in locations such as Dubai and New York City. Kader celebrates the International Day for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination annually, promoting values of tolerance and respect. In 2021, she organized an International Peace Walk in Dubai, further advocating for peace and unity across diverse communities through her initiatives.

By mid-day on 21 May 2012, 23 civilians, 19 rebels, and 18 soldiers were killed, with many soldier deaths occurring as the Syrian army attempted to storm Rastan. By day's end, the LCC reported 52 deaths, while SOHR confirmed 23 soldiers killed.

The **S.E.5a** was an improved variant of the original **S.E.5**, of which only **77** were completed before production shifted to the more advanced model. The main difference between early **S.E.5a** and late **S.E.5** models was the engine—a **200 hp Hispano-Suiza 8b** with a four-bladed propeller, replacing the **150 hp H.S. 8A**. In total, **5,265 S.E.5s** were built, mostly in the **S.E.5a** configuration. **Vickers** produced the largest number (**2,164**), while the **Royal Aircraft Factory** manufactured **200**. The S.E.5a addressed early power deficiencies, becoming one of the war’s most successful fighters.

In colonial times, the land that became Belmont was primarily used for tobacco production, owned by the Lorillard family, who were prominent snuff manufacturers. The family moved their tobacco operations to the central Bronx in the late 18th century, significantly expanding their property, which became known as the Belmont estate. After relocating to New Jersey in 1870, the city acquired part of the Lorillard's land for Bronx Park. The neighborhood and a street in the area are named after the Lorillard family, reflecting their historical significance in the region.

Kirk Penney was born in Auckland, New Zealand. He attended Westlake Boys High School. Penney played junior basketball and, at 17, joined the North Harbour Kings of the New Zealand NBL in 1998. He was named NZ NBL Rookie of the Year that same year. Penney played for the North Harbour Kings from 1998 to 2000.

*Archive 81* follows Dan Powell, a newly appointed audio archivist, who is contracted to review and catalogue a collection of audio recordings made by the Housing Historical Committee of New York State. Dan's work requires him to isolate himself in a remote location to digitize and organize tapes containing interviews conducted by Melody Pendras with residents of an apartment building. Throughout the process, Dan is contacted by his creepy boss, who insists that he continuously record himself. The tapes Dan is reviewing are 20 years old, dating back to the 1990s.

Khalifa Ezzat has provided community service at London Central Mosque for over a decade, offering a range of services to Muslims and non-Muslims alike. He has a successful track record in mediating marital issues and provides advisory services on personal religious matters, including marriage, divorce, and conversion registrations. In addition to Islamic marriage (Nikkah) and divorce (Talaq) procedures, he offers certificates in both English and Arabic. His services include one-to-one counselling, marital advice, and support for individuals navigating personal and religious challenges.

According to the 2011 census, Kupwara district had a population of 870,354, ranking 466th in India out of 640 districts. The population density was 366 inhabitants per square kilometre, with a growth rate of 34.62% from 2001–2011. The sex ratio stood at 835 females per 1000 males, and the literacy rate was 64.51%. Scheduled Castes constituted 0.01% of the population, while Scheduled Tribes accounted for 8.08%. The majority of the population resides in rural areas, with agriculture being the primary occupation.

Valentina Cortese was born in Milan, Italy, on January 1, 1923, to Olga Cortese and Napoleone Rossi di Coenzo, of noble origins. Before marrying Richard Basehart, Cortese had a long-term affair with Victor de Sabata, a married Italian conductor and composer three decades her senior. She left for Hollywood in large part to break off their intense relationship. In 1951, she married her *The House on Telegraph Hill* co-star, Richard Basehart, with whom she had a son, Jackie Basehart. The couple divorced in 1960, and Cortese never remarried. Jackie Basehart died in Milan in 2015, predeceasing his mother.

The **E (New York City Subway service)** began operations on August 19, 1933, when the first segment of the IND Queens Boulevard Line opened from 50th Street/8th Avenue to Roosevelt Avenue, six days after the Crosstown Line's Queens Plaza–Nassau Avenue segment debuted. Initially, the E service ran via the Queens Boulevard Line's **local tracks** between Roosevelt Avenue and Hudson Terminal (now WTC) because the express tracks were designed to lead only into Manhattan, while locals connected to Brooklyn. Weekday rush-hour service operated **every 7.5 minutes**, matching the Saturday morning rush frequency, while Sunday mornings saw trains every **15 minutes**. By the mid-1930s, rush-hour service expanded to **three- or four-car trains** to accommodate demand. The line's early design and ridership patterns—where express trains were overcrowded despite limited time savings—highlighted the need for later adjustments, such as the 1955 11th Street Connection, which integrated local tracks into Manhattan service.  

*(Word count: 150)*

Adar Friedmann began practicing gymnastics at a young age and has expressed that her experiences in the sport have been both stressful and rewarding. Her main ambition is to compete in the Olympic Games, aiming for Paris 2024, with a strong admiration for her idol in rhythmic gymnastics.

Jack O'Connor, associated with the club St. Mary's, won the All-Ireland Senior Football Championship with Kerry on three occasions during his first term as manager, specifically in 2004, 2006, and 2007. His first two All-Ireland victories were against the team of Mayo. In the same years as their All-Ireland triumphs, Kerry also secured the National Football League titles. Following the 2006 victory, county board chairman Seán Walsh expressed a desire for O'Connor to continue as manager. However, on October 1, 2007, O'Connor and his backroom staff, which included selectors Ger O'Keeffe, Johnny Culloty, and trainer Pat Flanagan, stepped down from their positions, citing personal reasons for their decision. Walsh's reaction to O'Connor's departure indicated disappointment, as he had hoped O'Connor would nurture the emerging talent in the county. During his tenure, O'Connor led Kerry to three All-Ireland titles and was the last manager before him to reach three finals was Eamonn Fitzmaurice.

Jiang Mianheng, born in 1952, is one of the co-founders of the Shanghai-based Grace Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation. Neil Bush, son of former U.S. President George H.W. Bush and brother of former U.S. President George W. Bush, was employed by Grace Semiconductor as a general consultant. Jiang Mianheng holds a doctorate from Drexel University, which he completed in 1991. His father, Jiang Zemin, visited Drexel University in 1997. Jiang Mianheng also served in a role in the Chinese space program. In 2007, he failed to win nomination as a delegate to the 17th Party Congress.
Moise Fokou was selected by the Philadelphia Eagles in the seventh round of the 2009 NFL Draft with the 230th overall pick. He played in 16 games that season, starting in 12 and recording 39 tackles.

The soundtrack for *Tetris* (Game Boy) was composed by Hirokazu Tanaka, featuring three background music options: "Type A," "Type B," and "Type C." "Type A" is based on the Russian folk song "Korobeiniki," also known as "The Peddlers," while "Type C" is an arranged version of Johann Sebastian Bach's *French Suite No. 3 in B Minor, BWV 814: Menuet*. An early Japanese release, dubbed the "Minuet" version (v1.0), used Bach's *Menuet* as "Type A" and was limited to roughly 25,000 copies. The iconic "Korobeiniki" later became the default theme in v1.1. Tracks from *Tetris*, including "Type A," appear in the *Super Smash Bros.* series, which features over 1,000 unlockable compositions.

The University of New South Wales (UNSW), in collaboration with NICTA, played a pivotal role in the development of the L4 microkernel family. Alongside Liedtke's original L4, retrospectively named L4 Version 2, UNSW implemented L4 on platforms such as MIPS64 and Alpha, with kernels written in a combination of C and assembly. These efforts included the development of two 64-bit L4 implementations based on the Version 2 API, which were used for teaching and research. After the release of L4Ka::Pistachio, UNSW discontinued its earlier kernels and contributed to maintaining the MIPS and Alpha architectures for Pistachio. UNSW also developed Wombat, a highly portable version of Linux running on processors like ARM and XScale, achieving context-switching costs on XScale processors significantly lower than native Linux. Notably, UNSW achieved the fastest-ever reported implementation of message passing on L4, demonstrating the microkernel's exceptional performance. These contributions solidified UNSW's role in advancing L4's portability, performance, and research applications.

In February 1930, Dominican President Horacio Vásquez was overthrown in a coup led by General Rafael Leonidas Trujillo, with support from Rafael Estrella Ureña. Following the coup, Estrella Ureña became provisional president. Trujillo and Estrella Ureña arranged to run together in the May 1930 presidential election, with Trujillo as the presidential candidate and Estrella Ureña as his running mate for vice president. Rafael Brache, a supporter of the coup, was appointed to a government position under the new regime.

In Venezuela, universities possess a degree of autonomy for self-governance, allowing them to secure funds through private donations and royalties. However, the ultimate responsibility for university management lies with the Minister of Education, who can oversee all operations, including budget, curriculum planning, and faculty selection. While universities can operate independently to some extent, they remain subject to government control, with the Minister having the authority to intervene in various aspects of university administration if deemed necessary, which can threaten their academic freedom and quality.

Catherine Jean Barton Para, known as Cathy Barton, was born on June 12, 1955, in Fort Benning, Georgia. As an "Army brat," she lived in Pennsylvania, Kentucky, Virginia, and Hawaii before her family moved to Columbia, Missouri, in 1967. She attended Hickman High School and graduated summa cum laude from Stephens College in 1977. In 1979, she received a master's degree in folklore from Western Kentucky University.

**Development**  
Netflix COO Ted Sarandos stated in 2015 that *Jessica Jones* was eligible for multiple seasons, noting that some *Defenders* series would "selectively have multiple seasons" based on creative pacing rather than a fixed schedule. He confirmed that the second season would air after *The Defenders* (2017), as the crossover's production affected Marvel-Netflix timelines. Showrunner Melissa Rosenberg, who expressed hope for a renewal before *The Defenders*, acknowledged the challenge of scheduling a potential second season around the team-up series. Netflix officially ordered *Jessica Jones* season 2 in early 2016, though production was delayed. Ahead of filming, *The Returned* alum Raelle Tucker joined as executive producer and writer, replacing Liz Friedman, while Rosenberg remained showrunner. Sarandos emphasized that renewal decisions would prioritize storytelling over rigid release timelines.

"Every Moonbeam, Every Feverdream," the 2022 EP by Bears in Trees, was preceded by the release of its first single, "Kind Love," on 7 October 2022, accompanied by a heartfelt music video reflecting on love and resilience. The second single, "Doing This Again!," followed on 28 October 2022, with its video uploaded to YouTube, recorded and edited entirely on the band’s phones. Released on 11 November 2022, the EP drew inspiration from the film *Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind*, with the band describing its themes as a journey through memory, love, and self-discovery. The project captures the chaotic yet tender essence of navigating relationships and personal growth, blending their signature introspective lyricism with a playful, DIY ethos.

By the end of the nineteenth century, Hughes' works became the largest in the Russian Empire, producing 74% of Russian iron by 1913. Following a period of relative decline in the early years of the twentieth century, the works experienced expansion during World War I. Many of the men who accompanied John Hughes settled in Hughesovka, bringing their wives and families. Over the years, the company trained a Russian workforce, while skilled workers continued to be employed from the United Kingdom, predominantly filling technical, engineering, and managerial positions. Many of these workers were Welsh. The expatriate community in Hughesovka was supported with good quality company housing, an English school, and an Anglican church, fostering a vibrant community despite the challenges of the local environment.

Salesian College, Battersea has a complex history involving mergers and relocations. In August 2011, Salesian College and the John Paul II School, both located in the Wandsworth Borough, merged to create a new school. This new school, named St John Bosco College, opened on September 1, 2011, initially occupying the former John Paul II School site in Wimbledon. St John Bosco College moved to the old Surrey Lane site in 2015.

In the United States, driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs is illegal in all 50 states and Washington, D.C., with penalties varying by state. In Wisconsin, a first DUI offense incurs fines up to $811.50. Criminal offenses include operating vehicles, watercraft, or machinery while intoxicated. Similar laws exist globally, including in countries like Canada and the UK, where operating vehicles or machinery under the influence is also prohibited.

The Saudi Arabian-led intervention in Yemen, known as Operation Decisive Storm, began on 26 March 2015, with Saudi F-15S aircraft and other Arab coalition assets launching airstrikes against Houthi and Yemeni Army forces. The early strikes targeted air defense sites, military headquarters, ballistic missile depots, launchers, and military airports to establish air superiority. During the opening day of the campaign, a Saudi F-15S crashed into the Gulf of Aden after circling over the sea. The two pilots ejected safely and were rescued by a USAF HH-60G rescue helicopter. Arab coalition reports attributed the crash to non-combat-related causes, while Houthi and Iranian sources claimed the aircraft was shot down. On 8 January 2018, a Royal Saudi Air Force (RSAF) F-15S was reportedly struck by a Houthi surface-to-air missile. A Houthi-released video showed the aircraft increasing speed and deploying flares before being hit by a projectile, appearing to sustain significant damage. However, Houthi media outlet Al-Masirah announced on 9 January 2018 that the F-15 had been damaged but did not crash. These incidents highlight the risks faced by Saudi F-15S aircraft during the ongoing conflict, which has seen the coalition engage in extensive air operations against Houthi forces since 2015.

Approximately 50,000 fans attended the Tri-Cornered Baseball Game on June 26, 1944, at the Polo Grounds, with many purchasing tickets through war bonds. Admission raised $5.5 million, while 5,000 injured veterans were granted free entry. New York City Mayor Fiorello LaGuardia pledged $50 million in bonds, and Bond Clothing Stores contributed $1 million for an autographed program. In total, the event raised $56.5 million for the war effort. New York Daily News sportswriter Jimmy Powers described the game as "the wackiest diamond battle ever conceived," highlighting the unique nature of the three-team exhibition game held to support the Allied forces.

In December 2007, Black Box Recorder collaborated with Art Brut under the name The Black Arts to release the single "Christmas Number One." The band later performed at a Nick Sanderson tribute concert in October 2008. It was announced on Luke Haines' website that their first headlining gig in five years would take place at The Luminaire in Kilburn, London, in February 2009.

The "Vanguard and neighborhood program controversy" at River Oaks Elementary School began when HISD board trustee Ron Franklin introduced a request on behalf of the River Oaks neighborhood to reinstate a neighborhood program in late 1995. Parents from River Oaks pressured the school to restore local enrollment, which had been discontinued years earlier when most families opted for private schools. Some Vanguard parents, many of whom were middle-class professionals from areas like West University Place, opposed the proposal, fearing that admitting affluent neighborhood children would displace students from across HISD and reduce the school’s diversity. They also worried that the River Oaks program would diminish their control over the school. At the time, the school had around 500 students, with racial demographics reflecting broader national trends (47% white, 27% Hispanic, 15% Black). Black, Hispanic, and white Vanguard parents protested the change, arguing it would undermine integration. Franklin accused them of a "them versus us" mentality, noting that most other Vanguard schools included neighborhood components.

**Tone**  
Greeny is renowned for its distinctive, versatile tone, characterized by a bright yet warm sound with exceptional sustain, as heard in Peter Green’s playing on tracks like *"The Super-Natural."* The guitar is equipped with "Greenybuckers," modified humbuckers featuring a neck pickup rewound with heavy formvar wire—typically found in Strat pickups—after an early breakdown. Green initially sought to emulate Eric Clapton’s blues tone but developed his own signature style, marked by clean, cutting timbres and dynamic subtlety. A critical quirk arose when the neck pickup was reinstalled backward, with its alnico magnet flipped—possibly an aftermarket mod—resulting in an out-of-phase middle position tone, described as "nasally" and unique. Kirk Hammett praised Greeny as "an anomaly" for its singular sound and heavily weathered history, including a severe accident where the neck broke into multiple pieces (reportedly 25). This combination of modifications and damage contributes to its legendary status.

Zharman is a shallow, hypersaline lake located in Kazakhstan. To the north, it is bordered by lakes such as Saryqopa Koli and Zharsor, while Lake Balkhash lies to the south. The coastline of Zharman is characterized by saline flats, and its shores and bottom are often barren. The meadows near the shore are utilized for grazing livestock.

Epipedobates machalilla is a slender frog with a snout-vent length of 14.4 to 16.0 mm in males and 15.0 to 17.6 mm in females. Its head is slightly longer than wide, and the tympanum is small and hardly visible. The forelimbs are moderately long with unwebbed fingers. The body is pale olive-tan with a dark brown X marking, while the upper lip is creamy yellow with pinkish shades, and the eyes are dark.

The Egyptian revolution of 2011 began on January 25 with mass protests and clashes between demonstrators and police, who used tear gas and water cannons. By January 28, police withdrew from the streets, and the military was deployed to restore order. Prison guards were later accused of torturing detainees, including a fatal case involving water hoses. On February 2, pro-Mubarak loyalists, including horse and camel riders, stormed Tahrir Square, attacking protesters with whips and swords in the "Battle of the Camel." The clashes left at least three dead and 1,500 injured. A fact-finding judicial committee, led by judges, later confirmed security forces' lethal violence, estimating 846 deaths during the uprising.

In 1996, House Majority Leader Dick Armey, originally from North Dakota, persuaded Kevin Cramer to challenge Earl Pomeroy for North Dakota's at-large congressional seat; Pomeroy won with 55.07% to Cramer's 43.22%.

During the Cold War, the United States and the Soviet Union invested heavily in propaganda, using radio and television broadcasting to spread influence and disinformation. Cold War themes endure in popular culture, with cinema and television continuing to reflect its narratives post-1991. The FX series *The Americans* (2013–2018), set in the 1980s, explores this legacy, following Russian spies Philip and Elizabeth Jennings living undercover in Washington, D.C. Ranked No. 6 on Metacritic’s 2013 Best New TV Shows list, the drama emphasized psychological tension over action, concluding its six-season run in 2018.

Carl Theodor Zahle was elected to the lower house of the Danish Rigsdag in 1895, representing the Roskilde district, and held his seat until 1928. He co-founded the Social Liberal Party (Det Radikale Venstre) in 1905. Zahle became a member of the parliamentary finance committee in 1902. In 1928, he was elected to the upper house of parliament, where he served until 1936.

**Material description**  
The term 'cement' originates from the Latin *caementum*, meaning "rough-cut stone," and describes a binding agent used in construction. When mixed with water, cement undergoes hydration, forming a hardened paste. Fibre cement enhances these properties by incorporating cellulose or synthetic fibres, improving tensile strength and durability. A carefully planned production process yields strong, long-lasting materials. Fibre cement is widely used in cladding, flooring, and other construction applications due to its resilience and versatility.

Only 77 original S.E.5 aircraft were completed before production shifted to the improved model designated as the S.E.5a. The main difference between the initial models of the S.E.5a and the late production examples of the S.E.5 was the engine installed; the S.E.5a featured a geared 200 hp Hispano-Suiza 8b engine, replacing the 150 hp H.S. 8A model. In total, 5,265 S.E.5 aircraft were constructed, with Austin Motors producing the largest number at 1,650 units, while the Royal Aircraft Factory built 200 S.E.5 aircraft.

After the October Revolution, Georgy Stepanov supported the Bolshevik political group and continued to serve in the Soviet Navy. He participated in the Ice Cruise of the Baltic Fleet from March to September 1918, which aimed to secure the Baltic Sea against German forces. From September 1918 to March 1919, he commanded the minesweeper "Sheksna." Following this, he held the position of chief of staff until March 1920, when he became the acting commander of the Onega Military Flotilla, engaging in combat against White Army forces on Lake Onega. In May 1920, Stepanov was appointed as the chief of the West Black Sea coast defense sector, serving in this role from June to August 1921. Subsequently, he was the head of the operational management of the Naval Staff from August 1921 to December 1924, and from December 1924 to October 1925, he served as the chief of the Black Sea Fleet.

Microorganisms, particularly baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae), reduce ketones to optically active secondary alcohols with high enantioselectivity. Non-Saccharomyces microbes can also be used, but accessing certain enantioenriched products remains challenging due to steric and electronic factors.

Jacqui Little played in the Women's United Soccer Association (WUSA), the first official professional women's soccer league in the United States, which began in 2001. As a sixth-round draft pick by the Bay Area CyberRays, she made 18 regular season appearances and started 16 games in 2001, contributing 2 goals and 1 assist. Little also made a brief appearance in the 2001 Founders Cup, where the CyberRays won the championship.

On June 4, 1991, Philip "Rusty" Rastelli was granted a compassionate release from the Federal Medical Center in Springfield, Missouri. He died of liver cancer on June 24, 1991, at Booth Memorial Hospital (now NewYork-Presbyterian Queens) in Flushing, Queens, at the age of 73. Rastelli is buried in Saint John Cemetery and Mausoleum in Middle Village, Queens. Joseph Massino succeeded him as boss of the Bonanno family.

In 1958, six years after the NRX accident, the Chalk River Laboratories experienced another serious incident involving the National Research Universal (NRU) reactor. On May 23, 1958, a fuel rod rupture led to a fire and contamination within the NRU building and surrounding area. The incident involved several metallic uranium fuel rods overheating and rupturing inside the reactor core. During the removal of a damaged fuel rod by a crane, the rod caught fire and tore apart. As the crane moved the larger portion of the damaged rod away from the reactor vessel (calandria), a ninety-centimetre (three-foot) piece of fiercely burning uranium fuel broke off and fell into a shallow maintenance pit. The broken rod falling into the repair pit resulted in a large fire.

The name *Eoporpita* derives from the modern genus *Porpita* (family Porpitidae), reflecting its resemblance. Mary Wade described it in 1972, interpreting annular float chambers as dactylozooids and gonozooids. *Porpita* belongs to Hydrozoa, with *Eoporpita* sharing chondrophore traits but differing in zooid structure.

Grandville Public Schools embraces the 'be nice.' program as a positive anti-bullying approach, developed by Christy Buck, the Executive Director of the Mental Health Foundation of West Michigan. Buck also serves on the Grandville Public Schools Board of Education. The 'be nice.' program has been adopted in various schools across West Michigan to promote mental health awareness and prevent bullying.

The University of Illinois Willard Airport was dedicated on October 26, 1945, and named after university president Arthur Cutts Willard. Airline service began in 1954, and the original terminal opened in 1960. By 1969, Willard was Illinois’ second-busiest airport. However, the Airline Deregulation Act of 1978 led airlines to prioritize larger hubs like Chicago O’Hare, where fares fell, reducing demand for Willard’s services. The current terminal replaced the original in 1987, but passenger traffic never recovered to pre-1978 levels.
Kathy Bardswick was born to Bill Bardswick and Annastasia (née MacNeil). She earned a B.S. in mathematics from the University of Manitoba and an MBA from McMaster University.

Released in 1999, *Mezzamorphis* marked Delirious?'s first major push into the mainstream, reaching No. 25 on the UK Albums Chart and topping the UK Independent Albums Chart. Lutz Eigendorf was born on July 16, 1956, in Brandenburg an der Havel, which was located in East Germany at the time. He began playing football for BSG Motor Süd Brandenburg and enrolled in the Children and Youth Sports School 'Werner Seelenbinder' in 1970. Eigendorf joined the youth academy of BFC Dynamo in 1972 and made his professional debut for the club in 1974, ultimately making 132 appearances in the East German top flight.

The gameplay of *Tales of the Heroes: Twin Brave* resembles the *Dynasty Warriors* series, featuring action-packed, beat-'em-up combat. Players control pairs of characters from 15 *Tales of* titles, battling waves of enemies and bosses. Antagonists like Elraine and Schwartz can be unlocked, offering additional challenges. The game supports both single-player and two-player modes.

Raymell Maurice "Ray" Rice was born on January 22, 1987, in New Rochelle, New York, to Janet Rice and Calvin Reed. When Rice was a year old, his father was killed in a drive-by shooting. Rice's cousin, Myshaun Rice-Nichols, was killed by a drunk driver when Rice was ten.

Sabeena Rafi was born on 6 October 1924 in India. She was the daughter of Shouryar Joseph and Anna. Sabeena earned degrees in literature and became an essayist and historian of Malayalam literature. She married Ponjikkara Rafi in 1963, and the couple did not have children. Sabeena Rafi passed away on 22 June 1990, at the age of 65. She was survived by her husband, who died later on 6 September 1992.

Civic engagement has been proven to be stimulated by online media, particularly through the use of social media platforms that facilitate public deliberation and enhance citizen education. Studies indicate that diverse media types, including video and interactive content, are effective in engaging citizens. The Internet allows for the creation of new social networks, broadening participation and fostering connections among individuals from various backgrounds. This expansion provides significant potential for cultural citizenship's previously 'ephemeral' practices to gain visibility and community-building opportunities. The explosion of creativity on the Internet can be linked to the democratization of content creation, which, when connected to formal institutions, enhances educational approaches and promotes a more inclusive civic engagement landscape.

Sant Hirdaram Sahib, born Ramchand on September 21, 1906, in the village of Bhirya, Nawabshah district, was the son of Indumati and Shri Sahajram Ji. He became a disciple of Baba Hariram, who named him Ramchand. In 1948, Sant Hirdaram Sahib migrated to India, initially settling in Jodhpur. His significant contributions led to the renaming of Bairagarh to Sant Hirdaram Nagar by the Madhya Pradesh government in 1995. The Jeev Sewa Sansthan, associated with his teachings, continues to run benevolent activities under his blessings. Rev. Siddh Bhauji, the successor of Paramhans Sant Hirdaram Sahib, has chosen a life of celibacy, dedicating himself to the welfare mission inspired by his Guru.

In the early 20th century, Hungarian migration to New Zealand saw significant developments. In 1909, István Rácz arrived in Tuatapere, Southland, and wrote letters to his hometown of Csongrád, Hungary. These letters inspired further migration, and in 1911, the Szivák, Kollát, and Kókay families joined him in Tuatapere. Over the next two decades, approximately 100 Hungarian families followed, establishing a small but enduring community. Among their descendants, Stephen Kokay, son of István Kókay, gained prominence in 1970 when he was elected as the representative of the Waiau district on the Wallace County Council. His daughter, Dr. Ilona Kokay, became a biologist and now teaches at the University of Otago, while his son, Les Kokay, is internationally recognized for his writings on Bob Dylan, particularly in *ISIS* magazine. Another notable figure, Mike Racz, achieved a Guinness World Record as the fastest oyster opener, showcasing the diverse contributions of Hungarian New Zealanders to their adopted homeland.

G. Scott Paterson, who received bursaries as a student at Ridley College and the University of Western Ontario, has maintained a strong connection to his alma maters. Serving as a Governor of Ridley College for over 24 years, he has supported numerous initiatives. In 2000, Paterson donated $1 million to the University of Western Ontario to establish the G. Scott Paterson Wing in the Schulich Medical Building. This facility, dedicated to advancing medical sciences, reflects Paterson’s commitment to fostering innovation and education. He expressed hope that his contribution would support groundbreaking research and inspire future generations of students in the field of biotechnology.

In the United States, most colleges and universities award letter grades ranging from A to F, often with plus/minus modifiers (e.g., A-, B+). These grades are converted to a GPA on a 0 to 4.0 scale, with 4.0 representing an A. The average GPA is 3.3 at private institutions and 3.0 at public institutions. A 4.0 GPA is achieved by earning straight A's in all classes.

The Island, a National Natural Landmark, is located on an isolated plateau at the confluence of the Deschutes and Crooked rivers in east-central Oregon (Context 10). This 208-acre (84 ha) site, surrounded on three sides by water, features cliffs ranging from 390 feet (120m) to 702 feet (214m) (Context 9, 10). The Island is approximately 1.5 miles (2.4 km) long. It supports one of the best known and least disturbed examples of native juniper savanna located within the Columbia Plateau (Context 10). The Island is also a designated Research Natural Area, jointly managed by the Bureau of Land Management and the U.S. Forest Service (Context 10, 15). The Bureau of Land Management closed the Island to the public in 1993. Between May and mid-February, only researchers are permitted to visit the Island.

The 2016 United States presidential election followed the constitutional requirements outlined in Article Two of the U.S. Constitution, which state that the President and Vice President must be natural-born citizens, at least 35 years old, and residents of the United States for at least 14 years. Candidates for the presidency typically seek the nomination of a political party, which is formally decided at national nominating conventions. These conventions are informed by the results of primary elections and caucuses, where voters cast ballots to determine their preferred party candidate. Primary elections, considered more inclusive and democratic, allow registered voters to participate in a secret ballot process. In the general election, held in November, voters across the country cast ballots for their preferred presidential and vice-presidential candidates. The general election ultimately determines the President and Vice President, making it the final step in the electoral process.

In 1964, Helmut Leherb was nominated for the Venice Biennale with his *Time Destruction Manifesto* (Zeitzerstorungsmanifest). However, his participation was blocked due to a government reshuffle by the new Minister of Education, Theodor Piffl-Perčević. Leherb's planned pavilion was to be a deep blue space featuring dead doves, umbrellas, and dolls attached to the walls. The Parisian art journal *Arts et Loisirs* ran the headline "First scandal of the Venice Biennale!" while the German magazine *Stern* featured the scandal on its front page, proclaiming "Surrealist Leherb: No white mice for Venice."

In August 2012, Uganda and Israel commemorated the 1976 Entebbe raid with a solemn ceremony at the base of a tower at the Old Entebbe Airport, where Yonatan Netanyahu, the commander of the elite Israeli commando unit, was killed during the operation. The ceremony, attended by Ugandan State Minister for Animal Industry Bright Rwamirama and Israeli Deputy Foreign Affairs Minister Daniel Ayalon, featured the laying of wreaths, a moment of silence, speeches, and a poem. The flags of Uganda and Israel were flown side by side, symbolizing the strong bilateral relations between the two nations. During the event, both countries renewed their commitment to fighting terrorism and working towards humanity. Forty years after the raid, in 2016, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, Yonatan Netanyahu’s brother, visited Entebbe to mark the anniversary of the rescue operation. His visit aimed to honor the legacy of the raid and strengthen Israel’s relations with sub-Saharan African nations.

On **August 20, 2008**, singer-songwriter **Will Hoge** was involved in a **motorcycle accident** in **East Nashville** after leaving a studio session for his upcoming album, *The Wreckage*. Traveling north on **Main Street** around **8 p.m.**, his scooter collided with a southbound van that failed to yield while turning. Admitted to **Vanderbilt University Medical Center** in **critical condition**, Hoge sustained severe injuries, including broken ribs, sternum, femur, kneecap, shoulder blades, and facial lacerations requiring **over 200 stitches** (sources vary between 100–300). His condition improved to **stable by August 22**. No evidence indicated **alcohol or drugs** were involved. The accident left him temporarily unable to play guitar and required multiple surgeries.

After being reformed in the 1930s, Herne Bay initially played at the Memorial Park, which had previously been a rubbish tip. The final match at Memorial Park was played on 7 May 1953, after which the club moved to Winch's Field. The ground is named after Winch's Brickworks, which had previously been on the site. The new ground cost £5,000 to build, and over 1,000 attended the inaugural game on 26 August 1953, a 2-2 draw with Tunbridge Wells.

The liver metabolizes alcohol at a constant rate of approximately 0.015% BAC per hour, or about one standard drink per hour. One British unit of alcohol contains 8 grams of pure ethanol, equivalent to 10 milliliters. This is approximately 0.35 US fluid ounces. Liver conditions or acetaminophen use can slow alcohol metabolism.

Each National Olympic Committee (NOC) could enter up to three athletes in the men's 1500 metres event at the 2012 Summer Olympics if all athletes met the A standard of 3:35.50. If only the B standard of 3:38.00 was achieved, an NOC could enter a maximum of one athlete. The maximum of three athletes per nation was established at the 1930 Olympic Congress. Qualifying times had to be achieved during the qualifying period, which spanned from 1 May 2011 to 8 July 2012, in competitions organized or authorized by the IAAF, its Area Associations, or National Member Federations. If no male athletes from an NOC met the A or B standards in any men's event, the NOC could enter its best male athlete in one event, excluding the combined events, 10,000m, and 3,000m steeplechase, under universality provisions.

As a result of the dissolution of the Netherlands Antilles in October 2010, Curaçao and Sint Maarten became constituent countries ('landen') within the Kingdom of the Netherlands, gaining autonomy over internal affairs. The planned new joint currency for these two countries is the Caribbean guilder, which was initially expected to be introduced in the second half of 2024, but is now expected to be placed into circulation on March 31, 2025, after delays.

The **Grodzka Gate – NN Theatre** has been implementing the **"Historia Mówiona" (Oral History)** project since at least **2009**, collecting testimonies to preserve Lublin's pre-war and wartime memory. The project is based on **recorded interviews** with inhabitants, documenting their experiences. Over **800 people** have shared memories, totaling **2,500–3,000 hours** of audio and video materials. Interviews focus on **pre-war Lublin**, Jewish heritage, and sociocultural changes. These materials hold **significant historical value** in Poland, serving as a key resource for education and commemoration. The archives are accessible **online**, including through the **dLibra digital library system**, which complies with **international metadata standards** like Europeana. The project underscores the Grodzka Gate’s role as a guardian of memory, bridging past and present.

During the punishment phase, Anand Lapsi testified that after dinner at Hideaway Pizza on July 22, 1999, a man wearing a red bandana and black T-shirt held a gun to his head near his white Mercedes in the parking lot, demanding, "Give me your keys and your wallet." Lapsi identified Julius Jones as the robber. Police later found Lapsi’s stolen car in the parking lot of Jones’ apartment, missing its stereo and speakers. On Rotten Tomatoes, *Daniel Isn't Real* holds an 83% approval rating based on 83 reviews, with an average rating of 6.8/10. The consensus calls it a "stylish, well-acted thriller" that explores mental illness with dark, psychedelic flair. Metacritic assigned a weighted average score of 65 out of 100 based on 18 critic reviews, indicating "generally favorable" reception. Critics praised its visuals, performances, and unsettling tone, though some found its narrative uneven.

Hibbertia bicarpellata is a small shrub typically growing to a height of up to 0.3 m. Its branches are wiry and covered with a combination of star-shaped hairs and longer, simple hairs. The leaves are linear to narrow elliptic, measuring 4–10 mm long and 0.5–1 mm wide, with a petiole about 0.5 mm long. The flowers are arranged singly on the ends of branches or in leaf axils, each on a peduncle 3–8 mm long. The bracts are linear to lance-shaped, 2–3 mm long, and hairy. The sepals are lance-shaped, with the outer lobes 3.5–4 mm long and the inner lobes slightly longer at 4–4.5 mm. The petals are yellow, egg-shaped with the narrower end towards the base, and 4–5 mm long. There are six to eight stamens arranged around the two carpels, each carpel containing two ovules. Flowering occurs from August to October.

Retaj Al Rayyan Mecca, a 4-star hotel in Mecca, Saudi Arabia, features 22 stories and 670 rooms and suites. The hotel offers a restaurant, fitness center, and meeting rooms. According to Trade Arabia, it marks the Retaj Group's first step in its GCC expansion plan, particularly in Saudi Arabia.

The 1974 season began with the Argentine Grand Prix, where Graham Hill qualified 21st. His teammate, Guy Edwards, was further back on the grid. Hill was running in 14th place when his engine failed. The Gibson Custom Shop launched the Collector's Choice series in 2010, featuring meticulous recreations of rare Les Paul guitars, including the famous "Greeny." An Artist's Proof version owned by Gary Moore was auctioned off in 2021. Gibson released the Gary Moore Les Paul Standard in 2017, which included features such as a carved Grade-AA maple top, Grade-A mahogany back, and a rounded '50s neck profile.

As of the 2000 census, Lower Nazareth Township, Pennsylvania, had a population of 5,463, with 1,973 households and 1,596 families residing in the township. The population density was approximately 743.2 people per square mile, with 2,031 housing units at an average density of 276.3 per square mile. The racial makeup was 97.6% White, and 1.2% of the population identified as Hispanic or Latino of any race. The township's suburban charm and proximity to retail centers have contributed to its appeal as a growing community within Northampton County.

Studies in Latin American cities have shown that consuming water without adequate sanitation services disproportionately affects low-income urban residents, who are more likely to suffer from diseases caused by polluted water. These "poverty diseases" include diarrhea, cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A, and intestinal worms, which are linked to long-term consumption of contaminated water. Health disorders such as malnutrition, gastrointestinal illnesses, and even cancer have also been associated with unsafe water. Causes include water pollution, lack of sanitation, and untreated water used for drinking or agriculture. One of the most notable outbreaks was the reemergence of epidemic cholera in 1991, which affected over a million people across Latin America.

In December 2007, Black Box Recorder teamed up with Art Brut to create the single "Christmas Number One" under the collaborative title The Black Arts. In October 2008, Black Box Recorder appeared at the Nick Sanderson tribute concert. It was subsequently announced on Luke Haines' website that the band would play their first headlining gig in five years at The Luminaire, Kilburn, London in February 2009.

Dorothy Paget, born in 1905, was the daughter of Lord Queenborough and a prominent figure in horse racing. She owned Ballymacoll Stud, a breeding farm located in Co. Meath, Ireland, and her horses won a remarkable total of 1,532 races. In 1943, she was recognized as the British flat racing Champion Owner. Among her notable achievements, her horse Straight Deal won The Derby in 1943. Paget was a leading National Hunt owner during the 1930s, 1940s, and 1950s, and she owned seven Cheltenham Gold Cup winners, including the legendary Golden Miller, who won the race five times from 1932 to 1936. Additionally, Roman Hackle and Mont Tremblant secured the Cheltenham Gold Cup for her in 1940 and 1952, respectively. Paget also had four Champion Hurdle winners, with Insurance winning in 1932 and 1933, Solford in 1940, and Distel in 1946. Notably, Golden Miller provided her with her only Grand National victory in 1934.

The Connecticut Raised Bill 1098 sparked significant controversy, with the Catholic Church stating that the bill would force it to alter its structure in violation of its religious principles. Sponsored by then-State Senator Andrew J. McDonald, the bill was described by McDonald as an attempt to create financial transparency in Catholic parishes. However, Bishop William E. Lori, then Bishop of Bridgeport, argued that the bill was unconstitutional and violated the First Amendment's protection of religious freedom. U.S. Senator Richard Blumenthal opened an investigation into the bill, aiming to examine its implications and ensure accountability. The bill was widely criticized for its perceived infringement on religious liberty and church governance.

Clayton, Missouri, is served by several major arterial routes, including Forsyth Boulevard, which becomes Olympian Way between North Clayton and University City. Forsyth Boulevard was part of the 1904 World's Fair route. Besides these major routes, all public transit routes throughout the state are also considered primary routes. Wydown Boulevard in Clayton was named a Great Street by the American Planning Association in 2010. The boulevard is known for its scenery and attractiveness for walking, jogging, and biking.

*Heroine* opened on September 21st and grossed approximately ₹7.5 crore on its opening day. The film garnered an average response, but still collected approximately ₹25 crore nett during its opening weekend. However, collections dropped significantly by 50% on Monday. There is no information about the total collection of *Heroine* by the end of its first or second week in the provided context.

Celtic's traditional rivals are Rangers, and together they are known as the "Old Firm." This rivalry is regarded as one of the most intense and famous football derbies in the world. As of May 2022, Celtic and Rangers have won a combined total of 107 Scottish league championships since the league's establishment in 1890, with all other clubs collectively winning just 19 championships. During the 2014–15 season, Celtic ranked first in home attendance in the UK. Historically, Celtic has strong associations with the Irish community and is traditionally supported by Catholics, while Rangers' supporters are typically Protestant, with ties to Loyalist and conservative ideologies.

Adar Friedmann, born in 2006, began practicing rhythmic gymnastics at age four. She has described the sport as both challenging and rewarding, emphasizing teamwork as key to success. Inspired by her idol Linoy Ashram, Friedmann aims to compete in her first Olympic Games at Paris 2024.

In 1985, Simon Baron-Cohen, Alan M. Leslie, and Uta Frith proposed that autistic children lack a theory of mind (ToM), struggling to understand that others have unique mental states, beliefs, and emotions. This was considered a defining feature of autism. However, Gernsbacher and Yergeau (2019) argued that this claim is empirically questionable and societally harmful, noting that classic ToM studies often fail to replicate and that difficulties diminish when autistic children are matched for verbal skills. A meta-analysis of replications revealed a small but significant pooled effect size (0.15), suggesting nuanced differences rather than a universal ToM deficit in autism.

The open-source tool XCMS was created by the Siuzdak Lab. In 2012, the lab developed a cloud-based data-analysis platform. XCMS Online added interactive PCA and multigroup analysis capabilities in 2014. It was demonstrated that XCMS Online could be used in a systems biology workflow.

Middelburg virus (MIDV) was first isolated from Aedes caballus mosquitoes in South Africa, where other species such as Aedes circumluteolus and Aedes albocephalus have also been found to carry the virus. Currently, MIDV is limited to Africa, but factors such as undetected circulation in human populations may contribute to its potential spread outside the continent. Sindbis virus, which is closely related to MIDV, has already spread beyond Africa and causes mild symptoms in humans. Horses cannot act as vectors for MIDV transmission, as they are amplifying hosts rather than carriers of the virus.

Newly hatched *Patanga succincta* (Bombay locust) nymphs are green and have distinct black markings. After several molts, their color may shift to brown or yellow-brown. Immature adults are initially pinkish with dark brown markings, which fade over a few weeks. By the following year, mature adults turn yellowish-brown.

According to the 2000 census, Exeter, Maine, had a population of 14,937, with 6,465 households and 4,034 families. The population density was 1,146.0 people per square mile (442.6/km²), and there were 9,349 housing units at an average density of 717.3 per square mile (277.0/km²). The racial makeup was 97.57% White, 0.17% Native American, 0.68% from two or more races, and 0.90% Hispanic or Latino of any race. [Contexts 2, 8]

The original name of the magazine was "Studime Filologjike," published by the Albanian Institute of History, which was established in 1946. This institute played a crucial role in shaping the academic landscape of post-World War II Albania, focusing on the study and promotion of Albanian history and culture. The original magazine featured sections on linguistics, literature, and folklore. After its first issue, the magazine was renamed "Studime Filologjike" in 1948 and was published under this new name by the Institute until 1957. In 1958, the magazine split into two separate publications: "Studime Filologjike" and "Bulletin for Social Studies." The latter focused on social studies and related disciplines, with article resumes provided in both Albanian and other languages to reach a broader audience.

Nnamdi Moweta began managing a stable of fighters called the 'Warrior Family from Africa' in 1985. This stable included Prince Mama Mohammed, who fought for the WBC Light Heavyweight World Title Championship in December 1985, and had a career record of 22 wins, 3 losses, and 1 draw. Moweta also managed Nigerian welterweight Young Dick Tiger, who was related to former world middleweight champion Dick Tiger.

The Akhil Bharatiya Akhara Parishad (ABAP), the apex body of Hindu saints and seers, does not participate in electoral politics but holds significant influence in Hindu society. While it has cooperated with the Vishva Hindu Parishad (VHP), a Hindu nationalist organization affiliated with the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS), the ABAP has openly criticized the VHP on various issues. Notably, the ABAP has refused to align itself fully with the VHP, maintaining its independence and distinct identity.

Breast cancer is the most common cancer among American women, with approximately 1 in 8 women (13%) developing it in their lifetime. Mammography, a specialized form of radiography using ionizing radiation, is the most widely used method for breast cancer screening. While mammograms are effective, they have limitations, including issues with specificity, as they can detect abnormalities that are not cancer, leading to unnecessary biopsies. Notably, 80% of biopsies performed after mammography are negative for cancer. Additionally, mammography is less effective in dense breasts, which can obscure cancer detection. Repeated exposure to ionizing radiation from mammograms poses a minimal risk, as modern machines use low doses (approximately 0.4 mSv per screening). However, the benefits of early detection outweigh these risks. For those diagnosed with Stage IV cancer, the average 5-year survival rate is about 31%, highlighting the importance of early diagnosis. Discomfort during mammography varies, with some women experiencing mild to moderate pain, though this is generally temporary and manageable.

"Terrace House: Closing Door" opened at the top of the Japanese box office, grossing ¥251 million (US$2.11 million) in its first two days. In its second weekend, advanced online ticket sales fell by 52% from the previous weekend, with 46,800 tickets sold. It was overtaken by "Big Hero 6" for the number one position. The film concluded its run with a total gross of ¥2.7 billion (US$22.5 million).

Polk City was settled in 1846 and incorporated on April 3, 1925. It was an early contender for the seat of Polk County. Isaac Van Horn named the town, presumably after Polk County, and is known for having gently rolling hills, reminiscent of his Boston home, which is unique in Polk County.

Kirk Penney re-signed with the New Zealand Breakers in 2016 on a three-year deal, marking his return after leaving in 2011. He debuted on 7 October 2016, scoring 15 points in a 91-84 win over Melbourne United. On 29 October, he scored 27 points in a 119-93 victory over Adelaide. Penney added 30 points in an 86-70 win against Brisbane on 6 November. During the 2016–17 season, he appeared in 28 games, averaging 20.0 points, 4.0 rebounds, and 2.3 assists, reaffirming his status as a key player for the Breakers.

Traditional Jukun beliefs and rituals are intricate, featuring sacred shrines for worship and sacrifices. They believe their migration from Yemen to Kwararafa around 596 AD was aided by giant crocodiles, which are revered and have a shrine at the Marmara pond in Wukari. The Jukun religion emphasizes the divine right of kingship, with the Aku Uka considered the son of a god. The sun god is paramount, and their faith includes communicating with the souls of the dead, reflecting ties to ancient Egyptian traditions.

The RADA Library, established in 1904, houses over 40,000 items, including more than 15,000 plays, making it one of the largest collections of rare and out-of-print English-language plays. Alongside plays, the library contains books on various aspects of theatre. Early contributions to the collection came from notable actors and writers, including Sir Arthur Wing Pinero and Sir Johnston Forbes-Robertson, helping to create a vital resource for students and theatre enthusiasts.

Joan Hooley first appeared on television in the 1950s. She was cast in the ITV hospital soap opera *Emergency – Ward 10*, appearing in the series in 1964 as surgeon Dr. Louise Mahler, who embarked on an interracial relationship with Dr. Giles Farmer (played by John White). Their storyline included a love scene that was rewritten for being "a little too suggestive," sparking controversy and press attention. Hooley later revealed that due to the backlash, her character—prominent until then—was quietly written out. The role marked a landmark moment in British television, featuring one of the first on-screen interracial kisses, though the scene was toned down before broadcast. Hooley's performance and the storyline challenged societal norms of the time, cementing her place in TV history.
